• Discount at: Rate implicit in lease (if determinable) OR Lessee's incremental borrowing rate

sections.title: ROU Asset Calculation
sections.content:
**LeaseCo signs a 5-year equipment lease:**
• Annual payment: $10,000 (at year-end)
• Prepaid rent: $2,000
• Initial direct costs: $1,500
• Lease incentive received: $500
• Discount rate: 6%

**Step 1: Calculate Lease Liability**
PV of 5 payments = $10,000 × 4.2124 = **$42,124**

**Step 2: Calculate ROU Asset**
Lease liability:           $42,124
+ Prepaid rent:             $2,000
+ Initial direct costs:     $1,500
- Lease incentive:           ($500)
**= ROU Asset:             $45,124**

sections.title: Initial Entry: Both Lease Types
sections.content:
**Using previous example:**

**Entry at commencement:**
Dr ROU Asset                    $45,124
Dr Lease Incentive Receivable*    $500
    Cr Lease Liability              $42,124
    Cr Cash (prepaid + direct)       $3,500

*Or already received, reducing cash paid

**Note:** The entry is the SAME for finance and operating leases!
The difference is in subsequent accounting.

sections.title: Finance Lease: Subsequent Accounting
sections.content:
**Income Statement: TWO separate charges**

**1. Amortization of ROU Asset:**
• Straight-line over shorter of lease term or useful life
• If ownership transfers or purchase option certain → use useful life
• Classified as amortization/depreciation expense

**2. Interest on Lease Liability:**
• Effective interest method
• Beginning liability × interest rate
• Classified as interest expense

**Result:** HIGHER total expense in early years (front-loaded)

sections.title: Finance Lease Example
sections.content:
**5-year finance lease:**
• ROU Asset: $45,124
• Lease Liability: $42,124
• Annual payment: $10,000
• Rate: 6%

**Year 1:**

**Amortization:** $45,124 ÷ 5 = $9,025

Dr Amortization Expense         $9,025
    Cr Accumulated Amort - ROU      $9,025

**Interest:** $42,124 × 6% = $2,527

Dr Interest Expense             $2,527
    Cr Lease Liability               $2,527

**Payment:**

Dr Lease Liability             $10,000
    Cr Cash                         $10,000

**Year 1 Total Expense:** $9,025 + $2,527 = **$11,552**

sections.title: Operating Lease: Subsequent Accounting
sections.content:
**Income Statement: SINGLE lease expense**

**Lease Expense:**
• Generally straight-line over lease term
• Total lease cost ÷ number of periods
• Includes: Lease payments + initial direct costs - incentives

**Balance Sheet entries still needed:**
• Interest on liability (increases liability)
• Payment (decreases liability)
• Amortize ROU as plug to achieve straight-line expense

**Result:** LEVEL expense each period

sections.title: Operating Lease Expense Calculation
sections.content:
**Same 5-year lease as operating:**
• Total payments: $10,000 × 5 = $50,000
• Initial direct costs: $1,500
• Lease incentive: ($500)
• Total lease cost: $51,000

**Annual lease expense:** $51,000 ÷ 5 = **$10,200**

**This is the target—now we work backward!**

sections.title: Operating Lease Year 1 Entries
sections.content:
**Year 1 Operating Lease:**

**Step 1: Calculate interest on liability**
$42,124 × 6% = $2,527

**Step 2: Record payment and adjust**

Dr Lease Expense               $10,200*
Dr Lease Liability              $7,473**
    Cr ROU Asset                     $7,673***
    Cr Cash                         $10,000

*Straight-line expense (our target)
**$10,000 payment - $2,527 interest = principal reduction
***Plug to balance (varies each year)

**Alternative presentation (same result):**
Dr Interest on Lease Liability  $2,527
Dr Amort of ROU Asset           $7,673
    Cr Single Lease Expense         $10,200

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Finance vs Operating
sections.content:
**"Finance = Front-loaded; Operating = Even"**

**Finance Lease:**
• TWO expenses: Amortization + Interest
• Interest higher early → higher total expense early
• Like owning with a loan

**Operating Lease:**
• ONE expense: Single lease expense (straight-line)
• Interest still calculated, but offset by varying amortization
• Like renting

**Both have same TOTAL expense over lease life!**

sections.title: Cash Flow Statement Impact
sections.content:
**Classification of cash flows:**

**Finance Lease:**
• Interest portion: Operating activities
• Principal portion: Financing activities

**Operating Lease:**
• Cash payments: Operating activities (generally)
• Short-term portion may be financing (policy choice)

**Key difference:** Finance lease splits cash flow; operating keeps it together in operating section.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Variable Lease Payments
sections.content:
**Variable payments based on INDEX or RATE:**
• Include in lease liability using current index/rate
• Remeasure when payments change due to reference change

**Variable payments NOT based on index/rate:**
• EXCLUDE from lease liability
• Expense as incurred (when incurred)

**Example:**
✓ CPI-adjusted rent → include
✗ % of sales rent → exclude, expense when incurred

**Exam tip:** Read whether variable payment is index-based!

sections.title: Lease Modifications
sections.content:
**If lease is modified:**

**Separate lease if:**
• Adds right to use additional asset AND
• Price increases commensurate with stand-alone price
→ Account for new lease separately

**Not a separate lease:**
• Remeasure lease liability at new discount rate
• Adjust ROU asset for change in liability
• If scope decrease: Reduce ROU proportionally, recognize gain/loss

sections.title: Reassessment and Remeasurement
sections.content:
**Reassess lease term if:**
• Significant event or change in circumstances that affects whether lessee is reasonably certain to exercise/not exercise option

**Remeasure liability when:**
• Change in lease term assessment
• Change in purchase option assessment
• Change in variable payments based on index/rate
• Change in amounts expected under residual value guarantee

**Use CURRENT discount rate for remeasurement (except index/rate changes)**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Initial recognition: ROU Asset = Liability + prepaid + direct costs - incentives
Finance lease: Separate amortization and interest expense (front-loaded)
Operating lease: Single straight-line expense (ROU amort varies as plug)
Both types: Same balance sheet treatment, different income statement
Finance cash flow: Interest = operating, principal = financing
Operating cash flow: Generally all operating
Variable payments: Index-based = include; not index-based = expense as incurred

title: Lessor Accounting: Sales-Type, Direct, Operating
description: Master the three lessor accounting models including initial and subsequent entries for each type

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: While lessee accounting changed dramatically under ASC 842, lessor accounting stayed largely the same as ASC 840. But you still need to master three distinct models: sales-type, direct financing, and operating. The exam tests your ability to determine the correct model AND record the entries!

sections.title: Lessor Classification Framework
sections.content:
**Step 1: Apply TOLES criteria**
(Transfer, Option, Lease term ≥75%, Economic ≥90%, Specialized)

**If NONE met → Operating Lease**

**If ANY met, then check for selling profit/loss:**

**Sales-Type if:**
• Fair value ≠ Carrying amount (selling profit/loss exists) OR
• Fair value = Carrying amount BUT collectibility concerns

**Direct Financing if:**
• Fair value = Carrying amount (no selling profit/loss) AND
• Collectibility is probable AND
• Any third-party residual value guarantee or variable payments

sections.title: Lessor Classification Flowchart
rows: Is FV = CV AND collectibility probable?

sections.title: Sales-Type Lease: Initial Recognition
sections.content:
**At commencement, recognize:**

**1. Net Investment in Lease (Asset):**
• Lease receivable (PV of payments + guaranteed RV)
• Unguaranteed residual asset
• Less: Deferred initial direct costs (if no selling profit) or expense (if selling profit)

**2. Sales Revenue:**
• Lesser of: Fair value OR PV of lease payments + guaranteed RV

**3. Cost of Goods Sold:**
• Carrying amount of asset - PV of unguaranteed residual

**4. Selling Profit/Loss:**
• Revenue - COGS

sections.title: Sales-Type Lease Example
sections.content:
**LessorCo leases equipment:**
• Fair value: $100,000
• Carrying amount (cost): $75,000
• Lease term: 5 years
• Annual payment: $22,000 (year-end)
• Guaranteed residual: $5,000
• Rate implicit: 8%

**Calculate Net Investment:**
PV of payments: $22,000 × 3.9927 = $87,839
PV of guaranteed RV: $5,000 × 0.6806 = $3,403
**Lease Receivable = $91,242**

**Selling Profit:**
Revenue (lesser of FV or PV): $91,242
COGS (CV - 0 unguaranteed): $75,000
**Profit = $16,242**

sections.title: Sales-Type Lease Entry
sections.content:
**Entry at commencement:**

Dr Lease Receivable            $91,242
    Cr Sales Revenue               $91,242

Dr Cost of Goods Sold          $75,000
    Cr Equipment (Inventory)        $75,000

**Net effect:** Profit of $16,242 recognized immediately!

**Initial direct costs (when selling profit exists):**
Dr Selling Expense              $X,XXX
    Cr Cash                          $X,XXX

(Expensed immediately, NOT deferred)

sections.title: Sales-Type: Subsequent Accounting
sections.content:
**Each period:**

**1. Interest Income:**
• Beginning receivable × implicit rate
• Increases receivable balance

**2. Cash Receipt:**
• Reduces receivable balance

**Amortization table same as lessee's liability table!**

**At lease end:**
• Receive guaranteed residual (or collect guarantee)
• Record return of asset at residual value

sections.title: Direct Financing Lease: Initial Recognition
sections.content:
**At commencement:**

**No selling profit recognized!** (FV = Carrying amount)

**Record:**
Dr Net Investment in Lease     $XXX
    Cr Asset (at carrying amount)    $XXX

**Net Investment includes:**
• Lease receivable (PV of payments + guaranteed RV)
• Unguaranteed residual asset
• PLUS: Initial direct costs (deferred, reduces yield)

**Profit earned through interest income over lease term**

sections.title: Direct Financing Example
sections.content:
**Same facts, but FV = CV = $91,242:**

**Entry at commencement:**

Dr Lease Receivable            $91,242
    Cr Equipment                    $91,242

**No revenue or COGS—just exchange of assets!**

**If $2,000 initial direct costs:**

Dr Lease Receivable            $93,242
    Cr Equipment                    $91,242
    Cr Cash                          $2,000

**The $2,000 reduces the implicit rate (lower interest income over time)**

sections.title: Operating Lease: Lessor Accounting
sections.content:
**Asset stays on lessor's books!**

**Each period:**

**1. Depreciation:**
• Continue depreciating the leased asset
• Systematic basis (usually straight-line)

**2. Rent Revenue:**
• Recognize straight-line over lease term
• Even if payments vary

**Initial direct costs:**
• Capitalize and amortize over lease term
• Same pattern as revenue recognition

sections.title: Operating Lease Example
sections.content:
**LessorCo leases building (operating lease):**
• Cost: $500,000; Useful life: 40 years
• Lease term: 10 years
• Year 1 rent: $40,000; Years 2-10: $50,000
• Initial direct costs: $5,000

**Total rent:** $40,000 + (9 × $50,000) = $490,000
**Straight-line revenue:** $490,000 ÷ 10 = **$49,000/year**

**Year 1 entries:**

Dr Cash                        $40,000
Dr Deferred Rent Receivable     $9,000
    Cr Rent Revenue                 $49,000

Dr Depreciation Expense        $12,500
    Cr Accumulated Depreciation     $12,500
($500,000 ÷ 40 years)

Dr Amortization Expense           $500
    Cr Deferred IDC                    $500
($5,000 ÷ 10 years)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Lessor Types
sections.content:
**"Sales-Type = Sell it; Direct = Defer it; Operating = Own it"**

**Sales-Type:**
• Recognize selling profit/loss immediately
• Like a sale with financing

**Direct Financing:**
• No profit at inception (FV = CV)
• Profit earned over time as interest
• Like a pure financing arrangement

**Operating:**
• Keep asset, depreciate it
• Rent income over time
• Like a true rental

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Third-Party Guarantees
sections.content:
**Residual value guarantees:**

**From LESSEE:**
• Include in both lessor and lessee calculations
• Part of lease payments

**From THIRD PARTY (not lessee):**
• Lessor includes in receivable
• Lessee does NOT include in their liability

**This can cause lessor to classify differently than lessee!**

**Example:** Lessee calculates PV at 85% of FV → Operating
Lessor (with 3rd party guarantee) calculates 95% → Sales-Type

sections.title: Collectibility Concerns
sections.content:
**If collectibility is NOT probable:**

**Lessor accounting:**
• DO NOT recognize lease receivable or sales revenue
• DO NOT derecognize asset
• Recognize lease payments received as deposit liability

**Reassess each period:**
• When collectibility becomes probable → recognize lease
• Use cumulative catch-up approach

sections.title: Lessor Disclosure Requirements
content: **General description** of leasing arrangements
**Lease income** by type (sales-type, direct financing, operating)
**Components of net investment** in sales-type and direct financing leases
**Maturity analysis** of lease receivables (5-year table)
**Residual assets** and significant assumptions
**Operating lease assets** by class and depreciation

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: TOLES met + FV ≠ CV = Sales-Type (profit at inception)
TOLES met + FV = CV + collectibility probable = Direct Financing
No TOLES criteria met = Operating (keep asset)
Sales-type: Immediate profit, expense initial direct costs
Direct financing: Profit over time as interest, defer initial direct costs
Operating: Depreciate asset, straight-line rent revenue, defer and amortize IDC
Third-party guarantees: Lessor includes, lessee excludes

title: Sale-Leaseback Transactions
description: Understand when a sale-leaseback qualifies as a sale and how to account for both qualifying and failed sale-leasebacks

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Sale-leaseback transactions let companies monetize assets while continuing to use them. But ASC 842 has strict rules about when to recognize a 'sale.' If the transaction fails sale criteria, it's just a financing arrangement! Understanding the tests and accounting for both scenarios is critical.

sections.title: What is a Sale-Leaseback?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Transaction where an entity (seller-lessee):

1. **Sells** an asset to another party (buyer-lessor), AND
2. **Leases** that same asset back from the buyer-lessor

**Business purposes:**
• Raise capital without losing use of asset
• Improve financial ratios (if qualifying sale)
• Tax benefits (in some cases)
• Unlock value of real estate or equipment

**Key question:** Is this really a SALE or just financing using asset as collateral?

sections.title: Step 1: Does a Sale Exist?
sections.content:
**Apply ASC 606 revenue recognition criteria:**

A sale exists if **control of the asset transfers** to the buyer-lessor.

**Control transfers if ALL true:**
• Buyer-lessor has present right to payment
• Buyer-lessor has legal title
• Physical possession transferred (lessor perspective)
• Significant risks and rewards transferred
• Buyer-lessor has accepted the asset

**If YES → Apply sale-leaseback accounting**
**If NO → Apply failed sale-leaseback (financing)**

sections.title: Conditions That PREVENT a Sale
sections.content:
**A sale does NOT exist if:**

**1. Repurchase option/obligation:**
• Seller-lessee has option or obligation to repurchase (not at fair value)
• Buyer-lessor can require seller to repurchase

**2. Leaseback is finance lease:**
• If leaseback meets ANY finance lease criteria AND
• Seller-lessee has significant continuing involvement

**3. Seller-lessee guarantee of buyer-lessor's investment:**
• Substantially all investment guaranteed

**These create continuing involvement → NOT a sale!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Is It Really Sold?
sections.content:
**"If you're coming back for it, you never really left!"**

**Red flags for NOT a sale:**
• Repurchase rights (can/must buy it back)
• Finance leaseback (substantial ownership risks remain)
• Guarantees (didn't really transfer risk)

**Think:** Did the risks and rewards REALLY transfer? Or is this just borrowing money secured by the asset?

sections.title: Qualifying Sale-Leaseback: Seller-Lessee
sections.content:
**If sale criteria MET:**

**1. Derecognize the asset**

**2. Recognize the leaseback:**
• Record ROU asset and lease liability (as normal lessee)
• BUT: Measure ROU asset at proportion of RETAINED RIGHT

**3. Recognize gain/loss ONLY on portion TRANSFERRED:**
• Full gain/loss NOT recognized
• Only portion "sold" to buyer-lessor

**Formula:**
Gain/loss recognized = (Sale proceeds - Carrying amount) × (1 - ROU/FV)

sections.title: Qualifying Sale-Leaseback Example
sections.content:
**BuildingCo sells building for $1,000,000:**
• Carrying amount: $600,000
• Fair value: $1,000,000 (at FV)
• Leaseback: 5-year operating lease
• Annual rent: $50,000
• Discount rate: 5%
• PV of lease payments: $216,474

**Step 1: Calculate proportion retained**
ROU (at normal measurement) = $216,474
Retained percentage = $216,474 ÷ $1,000,000 = 21.6%

**Step 2: Calculate gain recognized**
Total gain = $1,000,000 - $600,000 = $400,000
Transferred percentage = 100% - 21.6% = 78.4%
**Gain recognized = $400,000 × 78.4% = $313,600**

**Step 3: ROU Asset at retained portion**
ROU = Carrying amount × Retained % = $600,000 × 21.6% = **$129,600**

sections.title: Seller-Lessee Entry
sections.content:
**Entry for seller-lessee (continuing example):**

Dr Cash                      $1,000,000
Dr ROU Asset                   $129,600
    Cr Building                    $600,000
    Cr Lease Liability             $216,474
    Cr Gain on Sale                $313,126*

*Plugged to balance; approximates calculated gain

**Note:** ROU asset is NOT at PV of payments!
It's at the proportion of carrying amount retained.

sections.title: Failed Sale-Leaseback: Financing
sections.content:
**If sale criteria NOT met:**

**Seller-Lessee:**
• DO NOT derecognize asset (keep on books)
• DO NOT recognize sale or gain/loss
• Record FINANCIAL LIABILITY for proceeds received
• Account for liability using effective interest method

**Buyer-Lessor:**
• DO NOT record asset
• Record FINANCIAL ASSET (receivable)
• Account for receivable using effective interest method

**Essentially: A LOAN secured by the asset!**

sections.title: Failed Sale-Leaseback Example
sections.content:
**Same facts, but seller has repurchase option:**

**Seller-Lessee Entry:**

Dr Cash                      $1,000,000
    Cr Financial Liability       $1,000,000

*Building stays on books at $600,000!*

**Each Period:**

Dr Interest Expense             $XX,XXX
    Cr Financial Liability          $XX,XXX
(Accrue interest at effective rate)

Dr Financial Liability          $50,000
    Cr Cash                          $50,000
(Lease payment reduces liability)

sections.title: Sale at NOT Fair Value
sections.content:
**If sale price differs from fair value:**

**Sale price BELOW fair value:**
• Record difference as prepaid rent (asset)
• Amortize over lease term
• Reduces lease expense each period

**Sale price ABOVE fair value:**
• Record excess as additional financing (liability)
• NOT a gain!
• Reduces gain recognized (or increases loss)

sections.title: Not at Fair Value Example
sections.content:
**Building FV: $1,000,000**
**Sale price: $1,100,000 (above FV)**
**Carrying amount: $600,000**

**Analysis:**
• $100,000 excess over FV = additional financing
• True sale proceeds = $1,000,000
• Calculate gain on $1,000,000, not $1,100,000

**Entry adjustment:**
Dr Cash                      $1,100,000
    Cr Financial Liability (excess)  $100,000
    Cr Other accounts as calculated...

**The $100,000 extra is a loan, repaid through "above market" rent!**

sections.title: Buyer-Lessor Accounting
sections.content:
**If qualifying sale-leaseback:**
• Record asset purchase at fair value
• Account for lease as lessor (sales-type, direct financing, or operating)
• Recognize any selling profit/loss per lessor guidance

**If failed sale-leaseback:**
• Record financial asset (receivable)
• DO NOT record underlying asset
• Earn interest over the financing term

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Repurchase Options
sections.content:
**Repurchase option/right analysis:**

**Call option at FIXED price:**
• Generally fails sale test
• Seller-lessee controls whether to repurchase

**Call option at FAIR VALUE:**
• May qualify as sale
• Option doesn't provide economic benefit beyond market

**Put option (lessor can force repurchase):**
• Generally fails sale test
• Seller hasn't transferred risk

**Variable payments that guarantee residual:**
• May fail sale test if payments guaranteed

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sale-leaseback requires control transfer per ASC 606 to be a 'sale'
Repurchase options, finance leasebacks, and guarantees can prevent sale
Qualifying sale: Gain/loss only on portion transferred (not retained via leaseback)
ROU asset measured at proportion of carrying amount retained
Failed sale-leaseback = Financing (keep asset, record liability)
Sale not at FV: Above FV = additional financing; Below FV = prepaid rent
Buyer-lessor: Apply normal purchase and lessor accounting

title: ASC 740: Deferred Taxes Overview
description: Understand the asset/liability approach to accounting for income taxes and the framework for deferred tax assets and liabilities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Income tax accounting is one of the MOST HEAVILY TESTED topics on FAR! Nearly every company has deferred taxes. Understanding the difference between book income, taxable income, and the resulting deferred tax assets and liabilities is essential. Master ASC 740 and you'll conquer a major exam topic!

sections.title: The Core Problem
sections.content:
**Why deferred taxes exist:**

GAAP (financial reporting) and the tax code have different rules!

**Book income (GAAP):** Used for financial statements
**Taxable income (IRC):** Used for tax returns

**Result:** Revenues and expenses often recognized in DIFFERENT periods for book vs tax.

**ASC 740 solution:** Record the tax effect of these differences as:
• Deferred Tax Assets (future tax benefits)
• Deferred Tax Liabilities (future tax obligations)

sections.title: The Asset/Liability Approach
sections.content:
**Balance sheet focus:**

**Step 1:** Identify differences between book and tax basis of assets/liabilities

**Step 2:** Determine if differences are temporary (will reverse) or permanent (never reverse)

**Step 3:** Apply enacted tax rate to temporary differences

**Step 4:** Record:
• **Deferred Tax Liability (DTL):** Future taxable amounts
• **Deferred Tax Asset (DTA):** Future deductible amounts

sections.title: Temporary Difference Framework
sections.content:
**Compare book basis vs tax basis:**

**For ASSETS:**
• Book basis > Tax basis → **DTL** (future taxable)
• Book basis < Tax basis → **DTA** (future deductible)

**For LIABILITIES:**
• Book basis > Tax basis → **DTA** (future deductible)
• Book basis < Tax basis → **DTL** (future taxable)

**Memory tip:** Think about what happens when the asset is sold or liability is settled!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DTL vs DTA
sections.content:
**"Assets high, you'll pay; Liabilities high, you'll save"**

**Asset with higher BOOK basis:**
• More depreciation already taken for tax
• Less depreciation left to deduct → Pay more tax later → **DTL**

**Liability with higher BOOK basis:**
• Expense recognized for book, not yet deductible for tax
• Will deduct when paid → Save tax later → **DTA**

sections.title: Common Sources of Deferred Taxes
rows: Book accrues; tax deducts when paid
Book estimates; tax uses direct write-off
Book accrues; tax deducts when funded

sections.title: Example: Accelerated Depreciation
sections.content:
**Equipment cost: $100,000**
• Book: Straight-line, 5 years → $20,000/year
• Tax: MACRS → $40,000 Year 1

**After Year 1:**
• Book basis: $100,000 - $20,000 = $80,000
• Tax basis: $100,000 - $40,000 = $60,000
• **Difference: $20,000**

**Book basis > Tax basis → DTL**

**Why?** When asset is sold/fully depreciated, tax will have less depreciation left to deduct → higher taxable income → pay more tax.

**At 21% rate:**
DTL = $20,000 × 21% = **$4,200**

sections.title: Example: Warranty Liability
sections.content:
**Warranty expense accrued: $50,000**
• Book: Liability of $50,000 (estimated)
• Tax: $0 liability (deduct when paid)

**Analysis:**
• Liability book basis: $50,000
• Liability tax basis: $0
• **Book > Tax for liability → DTA**

**Why?** When warranty claims are paid:
• Book: Reduce liability (no expense)
• Tax: Deduct payment as expense
→ Future deduction = future tax savings

**At 21% rate:**
DTA = $50,000 × 21% = **$10,500**

sections.title: The Income Tax Provision
sections.content:
**Income tax expense has two components:**

**1. Current Tax Expense (Benefit):**
• Tax payable (receivable) for current year
• Based on taxable income × current rate
• Goes on balance sheet as liability (or asset if refund expected)

**2. Deferred Tax Expense (Benefit):**
• Change in DTL and DTA during the year
• Increase in DTL or decrease in DTA = Deferred expense
• Decrease in DTL or increase in DTA = Deferred benefit

**Total Tax Expense = Current + Deferred**

sections.title: Journal Entry Framework
sections.content:
**Standard income tax entry:**

Dr Income Tax Expense - Current    $XXX
Dr Income Tax Expense - Deferred   $XXX*
Dr Deferred Tax Asset              $XXX (if increasing)
    Cr Income Tax Payable              $XXX
    Cr Deferred Tax Liability          $XXX (if increasing)
    Cr Deferred Tax Asset              $XXX (if decreasing)

*Can be credit (benefit) if DTL decreasing or DTA increasing

**Total expense = Current expense + Deferred expense (or benefit)**

sections.title: Calculating the Tax Provision
sections.content:
**Year 1 facts:**
• Pretax book income: $500,000
• Taxable income: $450,000 (due to accelerated depreciation)
• Tax rate: 21%
• Temporary difference: $50,000

**Current tax:**
$450,000 × 21% = **$94,500 payable**

**Deferred tax (DTL increase):**
$50,000 × 21% = **$10,500**

**Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Expense - Current   $94,500
Dr Income Tax Expense - Deferred  $10,500
    Cr Income Tax Payable             $94,500
    Cr Deferred Tax Liability         $10,500

**Total tax expense: $105,000** (= $500,000 × 21%)

sections.title: Tax Rate Changes
sections.content:
**Use ENACTED rate expected when differences reverse:**

**If tax rate changes:**
• Adjust DTL/DTA to new rate
• Record adjustment through income tax expense
• In period the rate is ENACTED (not effective date)

**Example:**
DTL of $42,000 at 21% rate
New rate enacted: 25%

New DTL = ($42,000 ÷ 0.21) × 0.25 = $50,000
Increase = $8,000

Dr Income Tax Expense - Deferred   $8,000
    Cr Deferred Tax Liability          $8,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Intraperiod Allocation
sections.content:
**Tax expense allocated to different income statement sections:**

• **Continuing operations:** Most tax expense/benefit
• **Discontinued operations:** Tax on disc ops income/loss
• **OCI:** Tax on OCI items (separately stated)
• **Equity:** Tax on equity transactions (e.g., stock compensation excess)

**Key insight:** Not ALL deferred taxes go to tax expense!

Items recognized in OCI → deferred tax in OCI
Items recognized in equity → deferred tax in equity

sections.title: Balance Sheet Classification
sections.content:
**Under current GAAP (simplified):**

**ALL deferred taxes are NONCURRENT**

• Classify as noncurrent asset or liability
• Net DTA and DTL for same tax jurisdiction
• Present net amount per jurisdiction

**Do NOT classify based on when differences reverse!**

(This changed from old rules that required current/noncurrent split)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Deferred taxes arise from temporary differences between book and tax basis
Asset: Book > Tax = DTL; Book < Tax = DTA
Liability: Book > Tax = DTA; Book < Tax = DTL
Total tax expense = Current expense + Deferred expense (benefit)
Use enacted (not proposed) tax rate for DTL/DTA calculations
Rate changes: Adjust DTL/DTA immediately through income tax expense
All deferred taxes are noncurrent; net by jurisdiction

title: Temporary vs Permanent Differences
description: Distinguish between temporary differences that create deferred taxes and permanent differences that don't

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The key to income tax accounting is distinguishing temporary from permanent differences. Temporary differences create deferred taxes; permanent differences do NOT. Permanent differences affect your effective tax rate calculation—a common exam topic. Get this wrong and your entire tax provision is off!

sections.title: Two Types of Differences
sections.content:
**Temporary Differences:**
• Differences that WILL reverse in future periods
• Create deferred tax assets or liabilities
• Eventually, book income = taxable income (timing difference)

**Permanent Differences:**
• Differences that NEVER reverse
• NO deferred tax effect
• Items that are NEVER taxable or NEVER deductible
• Affect effective tax rate vs statutory rate

sections.title: Common Temporary Differences
content: **Depreciation differences:** Book (straight-line) vs Tax (accelerated/MACRS)
**Bad debt expense:** Book (allowance method) vs Tax (direct write-off)
**Warranty expense:** Book (accrued) vs Tax (deducted when paid)
**Unearned revenue:** Book (deferred) vs Tax (recognized when received)
**Installment sales:** Book (full profit) vs Tax (as collected)
**Pension expense:** Book (accrued) vs Tax (funded amounts)
**Prepaid expenses:** Book (asset) vs Tax (deducted when paid)
**Accrued liabilities:** Book (accrued) vs Tax (deducted when paid)

sections.title: Common Permanent Differences
content: **Tax-exempt interest:** Municipal bond interest (never taxable)
**Life insurance premiums:** Officer life insurance (never deductible)
**Life insurance proceeds:** Officer policy proceeds (never taxable)
**Fines and penalties:** Government fines (never deductible)
**50% meals limitation:** Meals expense limited for tax (partial)
**Dividends received deduction:** DRD reduces taxable income
**Stock-based compensation excess:** Permanent if forfeited (ASU update)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Permanent = Never
sections.content:
**"If it's NEVER taxable or NEVER deductible, it's PERMANENT"**

**Never Taxable (favorable):**
• Municipal bond interest
• Life insurance proceeds
• Portion of dividends (DRD)

**Never Deductible (unfavorable):**
• Fines and penalties
• Officer life insurance premiums
• Political contributions
• 50% of meals (partial)

**Permanent differences affect EFFECTIVE tax rate!**

sections.title: Temporary Difference Example
sections.content:
**Equipment depreciation:**
• Cost: $100,000
• Book: SL, 5 years = $20,000/year
• Tax: MACRS = $40,000 Year 1, then declining

**Year 1:**
• Book depreciation: $20,000
• Tax depreciation: $40,000
• **Temporary difference: $20,000**

**This WILL reverse!** In later years:
• Tax depreciation < Book depreciation
• Eventually total = $100,000 for both

**DTL = $20,000 × 21% = $4,200**

sections.title: Permanent Difference Example
sections.content:
**Municipal bond interest income:**

**Year 1:**
• Book income includes: $50,000 municipal bond interest
• Taxable income: $0 from this source

**This is PERMANENT!**
• Municipal bond interest is NEVER taxable
• No reversal will ever occur
• No deferred tax created

**Effect on tax provision:**
• Reduces taxable income permanently
• Lowers effective tax rate vs statutory rate

sections.title: Calculating Taxable Income
sections.content:
**Start with pretax book income:**

**Add/subtract temporary differences:**
• Book income > Taxable: Add back (will reverse as deduction)
• Book income < Taxable: Subtract (will reverse as income)

**Add/subtract permanent differences:**
• Never deductible: Add back
• Never taxable: Subtract

**= Taxable Income**

**Only TEMPORARY differences affect DTL/DTA!**

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**TaxCo Year 1:**
• Pretax book income: $500,000
• Municipal bond interest: $20,000 (included in book)
• Fines paid: $5,000 (deducted for book)
• Excess tax depreciation: $30,000
• Warranty accrual (book only): $15,000

**Taxable Income Calculation:**
Pretax book income:          $500,000
Municipal interest (never taxable): ($20,000)
Fines (never deductible):       $5,000
Excess depreciation (temp):    ($30,000)
Warranty (temp):               $15,000
**Taxable income:            $470,000**

sections.title: Deferred Tax from Example
sections.content:
**From previous example at 21%:**

**Temporary differences only:**
• Depreciation: $30,000 → DTL $6,300
• Warranty: $15,000 → DTA $3,150

**Net deferred tax:**
DTL $6,300 - DTA $3,150 = **Net DTL $3,150**

**Permanent differences:**
• Municipal interest: NO deferred tax
• Fines: NO deferred tax

**Current tax:**
$470,000 × 21% = $98,700

**Total tax expense:**
$98,700 + $3,150 = **$101,850**

sections.title: Effective Tax Rate Reconciliation
sections.content:
**Disclosure requirement: Reconcile statutory to effective rate**

**Format:**
Statutory rate × Pretax income = Expected tax

**Adjust for permanent differences:**
+ Nondeductible expenses
- Tax-exempt income
± Credits and other items

**= Actual tax expense**
**Effective rate = Actual tax expense ÷ Pretax income**

sections.title: Rate Reconciliation Example
sections.content:
**Continuing TaxCo example:**

**Expected tax at statutory rate:**
$500,000 × 21% = $105,000

**Permanent adjustments:**
Municipal interest: $20,000 × 21% = ($4,200)
Fines: $5,000 × 21% = $1,050

**Actual tax expense:**
$105,000 - $4,200 + $1,050 = **$101,850** ✓

**Effective tax rate:**
$101,850 ÷ $500,000 = **20.37%**

(vs 21% statutory—lower due to tax-exempt income)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Originating vs Reversing
sections.content:
**Temporary differences have two phases:**

**Originating differences:**
• First appear (book ≠ tax for first time)
• CREATE deferred taxes

**Reversing differences:**
• Previously originated differences now reversing
• REDUCE deferred taxes

**Net effect:**
If originating > reversing → DT balance increases
If reversing > originating → DT balance decreases

**Watch the DIRECTION of change!**

sections.title: Special Items
sections.content:
**Items requiring careful analysis:**

**Deferred compensation:**
• Book: Expense when earned
• Tax: Deduct when paid
• **Temporary** – DTA

**Goodwill (tax-deductible):**
• Book: Not amortized (impairment tested)
• Tax: Amortized over 15 years
• **Indefinite temporary** – DTL (may not reverse for a long time)

**Prepaid income (deposits):**
• Book: Defer as liability
• Tax: Often taxable when received
• **Temporary** – DTA

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Temporary differences WILL reverse and create DTL or DTA
Permanent differences NEVER reverse and have NO deferred tax effect
Common temporary: Depreciation, bad debts, warranties, unearned revenue
Common permanent: Municipal interest, fines, life insurance premiums/proceeds
Permanent differences cause effective rate ≠ statutory rate
Rate reconciliation: Start at statutory, adjust for permanent items
Originating differences create DT; reversing differences reduce DT

title: Valuation Allowance & DTA Realization
description: Master the more-likely-than-not threshold for deferred tax assets and when to record a valuation allowance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: A deferred tax asset is only valuable if the company will have future taxable income to use it against! The valuation allowance reduces DTAs when realization is uncertain. This is a MAJOR exam topic and a common area of management judgment that auditors scrutinize.

sections.title: The Realization Question
sections.content:
**Core issue:** Will the company have enough taxable income in the future to USE the DTA?

**Recognition rule:**
• Record DTA for ALL deductible temporary differences
• BUT: Reduce by valuation allowance if realization is not MORE LIKELY THAN NOT

**More likely than not = > 50% probability**

**Net DTA = Gross DTA - Valuation Allowance**

sections.title: Sources of Future Taxable Income
sections.content:
**Four sources to consider:**

**1. Future reversals of existing taxable temporary differences (DTLs)**
• If DTL will reverse in same period as DTA → income exists

**2. Future taxable income exclusive of reversing differences**
• Profitable operations expected

**3. Taxable income in carryback years (if carryback permitted)**
• Can recover previously paid taxes

**4. Tax-planning strategies**
• Actions company could take to generate taxable income
• Must be prudent and feasible

sections.title: Evidence Evaluation
rows: Strong earnings history (excluding loss that created DTA)
Losses expected in early future years
Excess asset value over tax basis in appreciated assets
Future reversals of existing DTLs in same period

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**"MLT + N vs P"**

**M**ore **L**ikely **T**han not (>50%) to realize?

**N**egative evidence → Need valuation allowance
**P**ositive evidence → Probably don't need VA

**Weight objectively verifiable evidence more heavily!**

**Remember:** Cumulative losses = strong negative evidence. Hard to overcome without compelling positive evidence!

sections.title: Cumulative Loss Position
sections.content:
**Three-year cumulative loss = significant negative evidence**

**If company has cumulative pretax loss in 3-year period:**
• Difficult to support DTA realization
• Generally need objective positive evidence
• Future projections alone usually insufficient

**Objectively verifiable positive evidence needed:**
• Existing contracts
• Long-term sales agreements
• Scheduled reversal of DTLs

sections.title: Recording Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**LossCo has:**
• Gross DTA: $500,000
• Three years of cumulative losses
• No DTLs expected to reverse
• Limited carryforward period remaining

**Analysis:**
• Negative evidence: Cumulative losses, short carryforward
• Positive evidence: Limited
• Conclusion: Full VA needed

**Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Expense - Deferred  $500,000
    Cr Valuation Allowance           $500,000

**Balance Sheet:**
DTA (Gross):              $500,000
Less: Valuation Allowance ($500,000)
**Net DTA:                       $0**

sections.title: Partial Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**PartialCo has:**
• Gross DTA: $300,000 (NOL carryforward)
• DTL: $100,000 (reverses in carryforward period)
• No other taxable income sources

**Analysis:**
• DTA supportable: $100,000 (offset by DTL reversal)
• DTA not supportable: $200,000

**Valuation Allowance needed: $200,000**

**Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Expense - Deferred  $200,000
    Cr Valuation Allowance           $200,000

**Net DTA: $300,000 - $200,000 = $100,000**

sections.title: Reassessing Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**Review at EACH reporting date:**

**If circumstances change:**
• New positive evidence → Reduce VA (benefit)
• New negative evidence → Increase VA (expense)

**Common triggers for reducing VA:**
• Return to profitability
• Winning major contracts
• New management with turnaround plan
• Expiration of circumstances causing losses

**Key:** Change in VA flows through income tax expense (continuing operations)

sections.title: VA Release Example
sections.content:
**Year 2: RecoveryCo becomes profitable**
• Prior VA: $500,000
• Now expects to utilize all DTAs
• Reversal appropriate

**Entry:**
Dr Valuation Allowance           $500,000
    Cr Income Tax Benefit             $500,000

**Effect:** Significant boost to net income!

**Note:** This is why analysts watch VA closely—releasing it can dramatically improve reported earnings.

sections.title: Scheduling Reversal of Differences
sections.content:
**Detailed scheduling may be needed:**

**Match timing of:**
• DTL reversals (source of taxable income)
• DTA reversals (need to use against income)

**Important considerations:**
• NOL carryforwards have expiration dates (pre-2018 NOLs)
• Post-2017 NOLs: No expiration but 80% limitation
• Character matters: Capital losses only offset capital gains
• Some items have specific carryforward periods

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: NOL Carryforward Rules
sections.content:
**NOL Carryforward rules changed under TCJA!**

**Pre-2018 NOLs:**
• 2-year carryback, 20-year carryforward
• 100% of taxable income offset allowed

**Post-2017 NOLs:**
• NO carryback (generally)
• INDEFINITE carryforward
• But LIMITED to 80% of taxable income

**This affects VA analysis!** Indefinite carryforward = more time, but 80% limit = slower use.

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Valuation allowance:** Beginning balance, changes, ending balance
**Nature of evidence** supporting VA release (if significant)
**NOL and credit carryforwards:** Amounts and expiration dates
**Tax attributes by jurisdiction** if material
**Uncertainties** about realization of DTAs

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Valuation allowance reduces DTA when realization not more likely than not (>50%)
Four sources of future taxable income: DTL reversals, future income, carrybacks, tax planning
Cumulative 3-year loss = significant negative evidence, hard to overcome
Weight objectively verifiable evidence more heavily than projections
Reassess VA each period; changes flow through tax expense
Post-2017 NOLs: Indefinite carryforward but limited to 80% of taxable income
VA release can significantly boost reported earnings

title: Uncertain Tax Positions (FIN 48)
description: Learn the two-step recognition and measurement process for uncertain tax positions under ASC 740-10

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Companies often take aggressive tax positions that may not hold up under audit. ASC 740-10 (originally FIN 48) requires evaluating and potentially reserving for these uncertain positions. This is a common exam topic and a real-world issue that affects many companies' financial statements.

sections.title: What is an Uncertain Tax Position?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A position taken in a tax return (or expected to be taken) where:

• The tax treatment is uncertain
• Tax authority may challenge on examination
• Ultimate resolution is unclear

**Examples:**
• Aggressive deductions
• Transfer pricing arrangements
• R&D credit claims
• State tax nexus positions
• Characterization of transactions

sections.title: Two-Step Process Overview
sections.content:
**Step 1: RECOGNITION**
• Is it MORE LIKELY THAN NOT (>50%) that position will be sustained?
• Based on technical merits, assuming examination occurs
• If YES → Recognize benefit (go to Step 2)
• If NO → No benefit recognized

**Step 2: MEASUREMENT**
• What is the LARGEST amount of benefit that is >50% likely to be realized?
• Cumulative probability analysis
• May be less than full benefit claimed

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Two Steps
sections.content:
**"Can you WIN? How MUCH can you keep?"**

**Step 1 (Recognition):** Is it more likely than not you'll WIN if audited?
• Technical merits analysis
• >50% = recognize something

**Step 2 (Measurement):** How MUCH can you recognize?
• Largest amount with >50% cumulative probability
• May not be full amount claimed

sections.title: Step 1: Recognition Detail
sections.content:
**More-likely-than-not threshold:**

**Assumes:**
• Position WILL be examined by tax authority
• Tax authority has FULL knowledge of all information
• Focus on TECHNICAL MERITS only

**Do NOT consider:**
• Detection risk
• Likelihood of audit
• Negotiation or settlement possibilities

**If threshold not met:** Recognize NO benefit from that position

sections.title: Step 2: Measurement Detail
sections.content:
**Cumulative probability analysis:**

**Steps:**
1. Identify all possible outcomes
2. Assign probabilities to each
3. Calculate cumulative probability (from largest to smallest)
4. Find largest amount where cumulative probability >50%

**Benefit recognized = Largest amount with cumulative probability >50%**

sections.title: Measurement Example
sections.content:
**TaxPositionCo claims $100,000 deduction. Assessment:**

| Amount Sustained | Individual Prob | Cumulative Prob |
|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| $100,000        | 20%            | 20%              |
| $80,000         | 30%            | 50%              |
| $60,000         | 25%            | 75%   ✓ >50%     |
| $40,000         | 15%            | 90%              |
| $0              | 10%            | 100%             |

**Step 1:** MLT sustained? Yes (75% cumulative at $60K level)
**Step 2:** Largest with >50% cumulative = **$60,000**

**Benefit recognized: $60,000** (not $100,000 claimed)

sections.title: Journal Entry Impact
sections.content:
**From previous example:**
• Deduction claimed: $100,000
• Benefit recognized: $60,000
• Unrecognized benefit: $40,000
• Tax rate: 21%

**Tax return shows:** $100,000 deduction (full amount)
**Financial statements show:** $60,000 benefit

**Entry for unrecognized portion:**
Dr Income Tax Expense            $8,400*
    Cr Liability for UTPs           $8,400

*$40,000 × 21% = $8,400 unrecognized

sections.title: Balance Sheet Classification
sections.content:
**Liabilities for uncertain tax positions:**

**Classified based on expected settlement timing:**
• Current: Expected to settle within 12 months
• Noncurrent: Settlement beyond 12 months

**Often noncurrent** due to lengthy examination process

**Presentation:**
• Cannot net against deferred tax assets/liabilities (different nature)
• Shown as separate liability (or reduce related asset)
• If refund expected, present as asset

sections.title: Interest and Penalties
sections.content:
**Policy choice required:**

**Interest on underpayments:**
• Can be classified as income tax expense OR interest expense
• Must disclose policy and apply consistently

**Penalties:**
• Can be classified as income tax expense OR other expense
• Must disclose policy and apply consistently

**Accrue when:**
• Position does not meet recognition threshold, OR
• For unrecognized portions of positions

**Begin accruing from date unrecognized position would have been due**

sections.title: Changes in Judgment
sections.content:
**Reassess at EACH reporting date:**

**Factors that might change assessment:**
• New court case with similar facts
• IRS guidance or rulings
• Expiration of statute of limitations
• Settlement with tax authority
• Change in tax law

**Changes recognized:**
• In period of change
• Through income tax expense (continuing operations)
• Unless related to prior period discontinued operations

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Statute of Limitations
sections.content:
**When statute expires:**

**If position was NOT recognized:**
→ Recognize full benefit in period statute expires
→ Tax benefit (reduces expense)

**If position was PARTIALLY recognized:**
→ Recognize remaining benefit
→ Also release any accrued interest/penalties

**Entry example (statute expires, $40,000 unrecognized):**
Dr Liability for UTPs            $8,400
Dr Accrued Interest               $X,XXX
    Cr Income Tax Benefit            $8,400+
    Cr Interest Expense (or Tax Exp) $X,XXX

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Tabular reconciliation** of unrecognized tax benefits (beginning to ending)
**Amounts that would affect ETR** if recognized
**Significant positions** for which resolution is reasonably possible in next 12 months
**Description** of years that remain subject to examination
**Interest and penalties** policy and amounts recognized
**Change in unrecognized benefits** reasonably possible in next 12 months

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Year 1: TechCo takes aggressive R&D credit position**
• Credit claimed: $500,000
• MLT to sustain: 60% (passes Step 1)
• Measurement: 70% cumulative probability at $350,000
• Benefit recognized: $350,000

**Year 1 Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Receivable        $350,000
Dr Income Tax Expense           $150,000
    Cr Income Tax Expense - Credit   $500,000*

*Or record $150,000 UTP liability

**Year 3: Settled with IRS at $400,000**
Dr Income Tax Expense (Benefit) $50,000 CR
    Cr Liability for UTP            $50,000*

*Recognize additional $50,000 benefit

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Two-step process: Recognition (MLT to sustain?) then Measurement (how much?)
Step 1: >50% likely to sustain on technical merits assuming full examination
Step 2: Largest amount with >50% cumulative probability
Assume examination occurs with full knowledge (no detection risk consideration)
Interest/penalties: Policy choice for classification (tax expense vs other)
Reassess each period; statute expiration = recognize full benefit
Disclose tabular reconciliation and amounts that would affect effective rate

title: Defined Benefit Pensions: Expense Components
description: Master the five components of pension expense and the calculation of the pension liability/asset

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Defined benefit pension accounting is one of the MOST COMPLEX topics on FAR! Companies promise retirement benefits and must account for this multi-decade obligation. The five-component expense calculation and funded status reporting are heavily tested. Master this and you've conquered a major FAR challenge!

sections.title: Key Pension Terms
sections.content:
**Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO):**
• Present value of ALL benefits earned to date
• Uses projected future salary levels
• This is the LIABILITY measure

**Plan Assets:**
• Fair value of assets set aside to pay benefits
• Held in separate trust

**Funded Status:**
• Plan Assets - PBO
• If positive = Net pension asset (overfunded)
• If negative = Net pension liability (underfunded)

sections.title: The Five Components of Pension Expense
sections.content:
**"S-I-R-A-G" (Some Investments Really Are Great)**

**S** = Service Cost (+)
**I** = Interest Cost (+)
**R** = Return on Plan Assets (-)
**A** = Amortization of Prior Service Cost (+/-)
**G** = Gain/Loss Amortization (+/-)

**Pension Expense = S + I - R ± A ± G**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SIRAG
sections.content:
**"S-I-R-A-G"**

**S**ervice cost → Expense (employees working this year)
**I**nterest cost → Expense (time value on PBO)
**R**eturn on assets → REDUCES expense (earnings on investments)
**A**mortization of PSC → Expense (plan changes from past)
**G**ain/loss amortization → ± (smoothing mechanism)

**Note:** Expected return reduces expense; actual return affects OCI

sections.title: Component 1: Service Cost
sections.content:
**Definition:** Present value of benefits earned by employees THIS PERIOD

**Characteristics:**
• Provided by actuary
• Based on current period's work
• Uses projected salaries (future pay increases)
• Always INCREASES pension expense

**Income statement:** Only component in OPERATING income

**All other components go below operating income (or in OCI)**

sections.title: Component 2: Interest Cost
sections.content:
**Definition:** Increase in PBO due to passage of time

**Formula:**
**Interest Cost = Beginning PBO × Discount Rate**

**Characteristics:**
• PBO is a present value → grows with interest
• Discount rate = high-quality corporate bond rate
• Always INCREASES pension expense

**Example:**
Beginning PBO: $1,000,000
Discount rate: 5%
Interest cost = $1,000,000 × 5% = **$50,000**

sections.title: Component 3: Return on Plan Assets
sections.content:
**Two types of return:**

**Expected Return (affects pension expense):**
• Beginning plan assets × Expected rate of return
• REDUCES pension expense
• Smoothed, predictable amount

**Actual Return (affects OCI):**
• What plan assets actually earned
• Difference from expected = gain/loss → OCI

**Formula:**
**Expected Return = Beginning Plan Assets × Expected Rate**

sections.title: Return Example
sections.content:
**Beginning plan assets: $800,000**
**Expected return: 7%**
**Actual return: $70,000 (8.75%)**

**Expected return:** $800,000 × 7% = **$56,000** (reduces expense)

**Actual vs Expected:**
• Actual: $70,000
• Expected: $56,000
• Gain: $14,000 → Goes to OCI (accumulated OCI)

**Effect on expense:** Only $56,000 reduces expense
**Effect on OCI:** $14,000 gain increases AOCI

sections.title: Component 4: Prior Service Cost Amortization
sections.content:
**Prior Service Cost (PSC):**
• Arises when plan is amended to increase (or decrease) benefits
• Retroactive benefits for past service

**Accounting:**
• Initially: Record in OCI (not expense)
• Subsequently: Amortize to expense over future service period

**Amortization method:**
• Straight-line over average remaining service period of active employees
• OR years to full eligibility (if most participants are inactive)

sections.title: Component 5: Gain/Loss Amortization
sections.content:
**Gains and losses arise from:**
• Actual return ≠ Expected return
• PBO actuarial changes (assumption changes)

**Accounting:**
• Initially: Record in OCI (not expense)
• Subsequently: Amortize to expense using CORRIDOR approach

**Corridor rule:**
Amortize only if accumulated gain/loss exceeds:
**Greater of: 10% of PBO or 10% of Plan Assets**

Amount over corridor ÷ Average remaining service years = Amortization

sections.title: Corridor Amortization Example
sections.content:
**Accumulated net loss in AOCI: $150,000**
**Beginning PBO: $1,000,000**
**Beginning Plan Assets: $800,000**
**Average remaining service: 10 years**

**Corridor = Greater of:**
• 10% × $1,000,000 = $100,000
• 10% × $800,000 = $80,000
**Corridor = $100,000**

**Excess over corridor:**
$150,000 - $100,000 = $50,000

**Amortization:**
$50,000 ÷ 10 years = **$5,000** added to pension expense

sections.title: Comprehensive Pension Expense Example
sections.content:
**Year 1 Pension Data:**
• Service cost: $80,000
• Beginning PBO: $1,000,000; Discount rate: 5%
• Beginning plan assets: $800,000; Expected return: 7%
• PSC amortization: $12,000
• G/L amortization (loss): $5,000

**Pension Expense Calculation:**

| Component | Amount |
|-----------|--------|
| Service cost | $80,000 |
| Interest cost ($1M × 5%) | $50,000 |
| Expected return ($800K × 7%) | ($56,000) |
| PSC amortization | $12,000 |
| G/L amortization | $5,000 |
| **Total Pension Expense** | **$91,000** |

sections.title: Balance Sheet Presentation
sections.content:
**Report FUNDED STATUS on balance sheet:**

**Funded Status = Fair Value of Plan Assets - PBO**

**If overfunded (assets > PBO):**
→ Noncurrent asset (Prepaid pension cost)

**If underfunded (PBO > assets):**
→ Liability (current portion + noncurrent portion)

**Amounts in AOCI:**
• Unrecognized prior service cost
• Unrecognized net gain or loss
• Reported net of tax in shareholders' equity

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contributions vs Expense
sections.content:
**Contributions and expense are DIFFERENT!**

**Cash contribution:**
• Increases plan assets
• Reduces pension liability (improves funded status)
• NOT equal to pension expense

**Pension expense:**
• Calculated using SIRAG components
• Affects income statement
• May be more or less than contribution

**Common exam trick:** Don't confuse contribution amount with expense amount!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Pension expense = Service + Interest - Expected Return ± Amortizations (SIRAG)
Service cost only component in operating income; others below or in OCI
Expected return (not actual) reduces expense; difference to OCI
Prior service cost: OCI initially, then amortize over service period
Gains/losses: OCI initially, then corridor amortization (10% rule)
Balance sheet shows funded status (Plan Assets - PBO)
Contributions affect funded status, NOT pension expense

title: OPEB: Other Post-Employment Benefits
description: Understand accounting for retiree health care and other post-employment benefits beyond pensions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many companies promise retirees health care, life insurance, and other benefits beyond pensions. These 'OPEB' obligations can be enormous—sometimes exceeding pension obligations! The accounting mirrors pensions but has key differences. Understanding OPEB is essential for a complete picture of employee benefit accounting.

sections.title: What is OPEB?
sections.content:
**Other Post-Employment Benefits include:**

• **Health care benefits** (most significant)
• Life insurance for retirees
• Legal services
• Tuition assistance
• Other welfare benefits provided after employment ends

**NOT OPEB:**
• Pensions (separate standard)
• Termination benefits (different accounting)
• Stock compensation (different standard)

sections.title: The APBO Calculation
sections.content:
**Accumulated Postretirement Benefit Obligation (APBO):**

**Definition:** Present value of expected future benefits attributed to employee service rendered to date.

**Key inputs:**
• Per capita claims cost (expected cost per retiree)
• Health care cost trend rate (expected increase in costs)
• Discount rate (high-quality corporate bonds)
• Mortality assumptions
• Expected retirement age and coverage period

**The APBO is the LIABILITY measure (like PBO for pensions)**

sections.title: Health Care Cost Trend Rate
sections.content:
**Unique to OPEB accounting!**

**Definition:** Expected annual increase in per capita health care costs

**Typically:**
• Starts higher (e.g., 7-8%)
• Grades down to ultimate rate (e.g., 4-5%)
• Reaches ultimate rate over several years

**Example:**
Year 1: 7%
Year 2: 6.5%
Year 3: 6%
...
Ultimate: 4.5%

**Impact:** Higher trend rate = Higher APBO = Higher expense

sections.title: OPEB Expense Components
sections.content:
**Same SIRAG components as pensions:**

**S** = Service cost (benefits earned this period)
**I** = Interest cost (APBO × discount rate)
**R** = Expected return on plan assets (if any)
**A** = Amortization of prior service cost
**G** = Gain/loss amortization (corridor method)

**Formula:**
**OPEB Expense = S + I - R ± A ± G**

**Key difference:** R is often zero (unfunded plans)

sections.title: OPEB Example
sections.content:
**Year 1 OPEB Data:**
• Service cost: $50,000
• Beginning APBO: $500,000; Discount rate: 5%
• Plan assets: $0 (unfunded)
• PSC amortization: $8,000
• No G/L amortization needed

**OPEB Expense Calculation:**

| Component | Amount |
|-----------|--------|
| Service cost | $50,000 |
| Interest cost ($500K × 5%) | $25,000 |
| Expected return | $0 |
| PSC amortization | $8,000 |
| G/L amortization | $0 |
| **Total OPEB Expense** | **$83,000** |

sections.title: Unfunded Plans
sections.content:
**Most OPEB plans are unfunded or underfunded:**

**Why?**
• No tax deduction for prefunding (unlike pensions)
• Health care costs harder to predict
• Companies prefer to pay-as-you-go

**Accounting impact:**
• Full APBO appears as liability
• No plan asset return to offset expense
• Greater balance sheet impact
• Higher reported expense

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: OPEB vs Pension
sections.content:
**"OPEB = Often Pays Everything in Benefits (no assets!)"**

**Key differences to remember:**
• **APBO** not PBO (Accumulated vs Projected)
• **Health care trend rate** not salary growth
• **Usually unfunded** = No asset return offset
• **Medicare eligibility** affects obligation

**Same SIRAG formula, different inputs!**

sections.title: Attribution Period
sections.content:
**When to attribute benefits:**

**Pensions:** From hire date to expected retirement

**OPEB:** From hire date OR date when service begins to earn OPEB, whichever is LATER

**Full eligibility date:** Date when employee has earned full benefits

**No attribution after full eligibility!**

**Example:**
If plan requires 20 years service:
• Hire age 30, retire age 65
• Attribution: Years 30-50 (20 years to full eligibility)
• No cost attributed from age 50-65

sections.title: Plan Amendments
sections.content:
**When OPEB plan is amended:**

**Increases in benefits:**
• Prior service cost (PSC) recognized in OCI
• Amortize to expense over remaining service period

**Decreases in benefits (plan curtailment):**
• Negative PSC (prior service credit)
• May result in immediate gain recognition

**Common trend:** Companies reducing OPEB promises
→ Watch for curtailment gains in practice

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
content: **Obligation and funded status:** APBO, plan assets, net liability
**Components of OPEB cost:** Each SIRAG component
**Assumptions:** Discount rate, health care cost trend rate, expected return
**Sensitivity analysis:** Effect of 1% change in health care trend rate
**Expected future benefit payments:** Next 5 years + 5-year aggregate
**Expected contributions** in upcoming year

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Medicare Coordination
sections.content:
**Medicare affects OPEB obligations!**

**At age 65, Medicare becomes primary:**
• Employer plan becomes secondary
• Employer cost decreases significantly

**Measurement must consider:**
• Medicare eligibility age
• Expected Medicare coverage
• Coordination of benefits provisions

**Result:** APBO often drops at Medicare age in projections

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: OPEB = Health care and other post-employment benefits (not pensions)
Uses APBO (Accumulated) instead of PBO
Health care cost trend rate replaces salary growth assumption
Most OPEB plans unfunded → full liability, no asset return offset
Same SIRAG expense formula as pensions
Attribution stops at full eligibility date (not retirement)
Medicare coordination reduces employer obligation at age 65

title: Contingencies & Commitments
description: Master the recognition and disclosure criteria for loss contingencies, gain contingencies, and commitments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Companies face uncertainties—lawsuits, warranties, environmental liabilities, guarantees. ASC 450 tells us when to accrue a loss, when to just disclose, and when to stay silent. Getting the probability assessment right is critical for proper financial reporting and a frequent exam topic!

sections.title: What is a Contingency?
sections.content:
**Definition:** An existing condition, situation, or set of circumstances involving uncertainty that will be resolved when one or more future events occur or fail to occur.

**Two types:**
• **Loss contingencies** – Potential liabilities
• **Gain contingencies** – Potential assets

**Key:** The outcome depends on uncertain future events

sections.title: Loss Contingency Framework
sections.content:
**Two criteria for accrual (BOTH required):**

**1. Probability of loss:**
• **Probable** – Likely to occur
• **Reasonably possible** – More than remote but less than probable
• **Remote** – Slight chance of occurrence

**2. Amount of loss:**
• **Reasonably estimable** – Can determine amount
• **Not reasonably estimable** – Cannot determine

**Accrue loss if:** Probable AND Reasonably Estimable

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: PE for Accrual
sections.content:
**"P-E" = Probable AND Estimable**

Both required to accrue!

**"P" without "E"** = Disclose (probable but can't estimate)
**"E" without "P"** = Disclose (can estimate but only reasonably possible)
**Neither P nor E** = Usually nothing (remote)

**Think:** "Physical Education is required" → PE (Probable + Estimable) is required to accrue!

sections.title: Accrual Entry
sections.content:
**LawsuitCo is being sued for patent infringement:**
• Probability of loss: Probable
• Estimated amount: $500,000

**Entry:**
Dr Loss from Litigation         $500,000
    Cr Estimated Liability           $500,000

**Financial statement impact:**
• Income statement: Loss of $500,000
• Balance sheet: Liability of $500,000

sections.title: Range of Estimates
sections.content:
**When loss is probable but only a range can be estimated:**

**If NO amount in range is better estimate:**
• Accrue the MINIMUM of the range
• Disclose the range in notes

**If a specific amount IS the best estimate:**
• Accrue that amount (even if not minimum)
• Disclose range if material

**Example:**
Range: $200,000 to $800,000
No amount more likely than another
**Accrue: $200,000** (minimum)

sections.title: Common Loss Contingencies
content: **Litigation** – Lawsuits, claims, assessments
**Warranties** – Product defect obligations
**Environmental** – Cleanup, remediation costs
**Guarantees** – Debt guarantees, indemnifications
**Self-insurance** – Uninsured portions of claims
**Collectibility** – Uncollectible receivables
**Expropriation** – Asset seizure by government

sections.title: Warranty Example
sections.content:
**ProductCo sells 10,000 widgets with 1-year warranty:**
• Sales price: $100 each
• Historical warranty cost: 2% of sales
• Sales this year: $1,000,000

**Analysis:**
• Probability: Probable (warranties will be claimed)
• Estimate: $1,000,000 × 2% = $20,000

**Entry at sale:**
Dr Warranty Expense             $20,000
    Cr Estimated Warranty Liability  $20,000

**When claims paid:**
Dr Estimated Warranty Liability  $X,XXX
    Cr Cash/Inventory               $X,XXX

sections.title: Gain Contingencies
sections.content:
**Asymmetric treatment!**

**Gain contingencies are NEVER accrued before realization!**

**Why?** Conservatism—don't count chickens before they hatch

**Treatment:**
• May disclose if probable and amount can be estimated
• Do NOT mislead as to likelihood of realization
• Recognize gain only when realized (contingency resolved)

**Example:** Lawsuit where company is plaintiff
→ Don't accrue until judgment collected

sections.title: Subsequent Events
sections.content:
**Events after balance sheet date but before issuance:**

**Type I (Recognized):**
• Provide additional evidence about conditions at balance sheet date
• ADJUST financial statements
• Example: Settlement of lawsuit that was pending at year-end

**Type II (Disclosed):**
• Conditions arose AFTER balance sheet date
• DISCLOSE but don't adjust
• Example: Major casualty loss after year-end

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**For loss contingencies disclosed but not accrued:**

**Required disclosures:**
• Nature of the contingency
• Estimate of possible loss (or range)
• Statement that estimate cannot be made (if applicable)

**For accrued losses:**
• Nature of contingency
• Amount accrued (if not obvious from financial statements)
• Possible additional loss beyond amount accrued

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Remote Guarantees
sections.content:
**Exception to "no disclosure for remote":**

**Guarantees of indebtedness of others:**
• Disclose even if remote!
• FIN 45 requires disclosure of:
  - Nature of guarantee
  - Maximum potential payment
  - Carrying amount of liability
  - Recourse provisions

**Why?** Users need to know about guarantee exposure regardless of probability

sections.title: Commitments
sections.content:
**Different from contingencies!**

**Commitments:** Obligations to do something in the future

**Examples:**
• Purchase commitments (contracts to buy inventory)
• Operating lease commitments (now on balance sheet under ASC 842)
• Construction commitments
• Employment contracts

**Accounting:**
• Generally disclosed only (no accrual until goods/services received)
• Exception: Loss on firm purchase commitment → accrue if material

sections.title: Purchase Commitment Loss
sections.content:
**CommitCo contracted to buy 1,000 units at $50 each:**
• Contract is firm and noncancelable
• Market price at year-end: $35 per unit
• Loss: ($50 - $35) × 1,000 = $15,000

**Entry:**
Dr Loss on Purchase Commitment   $15,000
    Cr Estimated Liability           $15,000

**When goods received:**
Dr Inventory                     $35,000
Dr Estimated Liability           $15,000
    Cr Cash                          $50,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Accrue loss contingency if BOTH probable AND reasonably estimable
Reasonably possible → Disclose only
Remote → Generally no disclosure (except guarantees)
Range with no best estimate → Accrue minimum
Gain contingencies → NEVER accrue before realization
Guarantees must be disclosed even if remote
Purchase commitment losses → Accrue if material

title: Earnings Per Share: Basic & Diluted
description: Master the calculation of basic and diluted EPS including the treatment of stock options, convertibles, and contingent shares

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Earnings Per Share is one of the MOST SCRUTINIZED metrics by investors and analysts! ASC 260 requires public companies to report both basic and diluted EPS. The calculations involve weighted averages, treasury stock method, if-converted method, and antidilution tests. EPS calculations are a FAR exam staple!

sections.title: Basic EPS Formula
sections.content:
**Basic Earnings Per Share:**

**Numerator:** Net Income - Preferred Dividends
**Denominator:** Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding

$$\text{Basic EPS} = \frac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Weighted Avg Common Shares}}$$

**Key points:**
• Subtract preferred dividends (whether declared or not for cumulative)
• Use weighted average shares (not year-end shares)
• Stock dividends/splits retroactively adjust ALL prior periods

sections.title: Weighted Average Shares
sections.content:
**ShareCo share activity:**
• January 1: 100,000 shares outstanding
• April 1: Issue 20,000 shares
• October 1: Repurchase 12,000 shares (treasury)

**Weighted Average Calculation:**

| Period | Shares | Months | Weighted |
|--------|--------|--------|----------|
| Jan-Mar | 100,000 | 3/12 | 25,000 |
| Apr-Sep | 120,000 | 6/12 | 60,000 |
| Oct-Dec | 108,000 | 3/12 | 27,000 |
| **Total** | | | **112,000** |

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Critical rule:** Treat as if occurred at BEGINNING of earliest period presented!

**Stock dividend/split:**
• Retroactively adjust ALL weighted average calculations
• Adjust prior period EPS for comparability
• No weighting for timing during year

**Example:**
10% stock dividend on September 1
→ Multiply ALL prior shares by 1.10
→ Adjust prior year EPS by dividing by 1.10

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Stock Splits
sections.content:
**"Splits go BACK!"**

Stock dividends and splits are treated as if they happened at the BEGINNING of the EARLIEST period.

**Why?** For comparability—you want apples to apples across periods.

**Easy adjustment:** If 2-for-1 split occurs mid-year:
• Multiply ALL prior shares by 2
• Don't weight the split for timing

sections.title: Diluted EPS: The Concept
sections.content:
**Diluted EPS shows:** What EPS would be if all dilutive securities were converted/exercised.

**Potentially dilutive securities:**
• Stock options and warrants
• Convertible bonds
• Convertible preferred stock
• Contingently issuable shares

**Diluted EPS formula:**

$$\text{Diluted EPS} = \frac{\text{Adjusted Net Income}}{\text{Adjusted Weighted Avg Shares}}$$

**Include ONLY if dilutive (reduces EPS)!**

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method: Options/Warrants
sections.content:
**For stock options and warrants:**

**Assume:**
1. Options exercised at beginning of year (or grant date if later)
2. Company receives exercise proceeds
3. Proceeds used to repurchase shares at AVERAGE market price
4. Net INCREMENTAL shares added to denominator

**Formula:**
$$\text{Incremental Shares} = \text{Shares from Options} - \text{Shares Repurchased}$$

**Only dilutive if:** Exercise price < Average market price (options "in the money")

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method Example
sections.content:
**Options outstanding: 10,000**
**Exercise price: $20**
**Average market price: $50**

**Step 1: Shares from exercise**
10,000 options = 10,000 shares

**Step 2: Proceeds received**
10,000 × $20 = $200,000

**Step 3: Treasury shares repurchased**
$200,000 ÷ $50 = 4,000 shares

**Step 4: Incremental shares**
10,000 - 4,000 = **6,000 incremental shares**

**Add 6,000 to denominator for diluted EPS**

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Bonds
sections.content:
**For convertible bonds:**

**Assume converted at beginning of year (or issue date if later):**

**Numerator adjustment:**
+ Interest expense avoided (after tax)

**Denominator adjustment:**
+ Shares that would be issued upon conversion

**Formula:**
$$\text{Interest Saved (net)} = \text{Interest Expense} \times (1 - \text{Tax Rate})$$

sections.title: Convertible Bond Example
sections.content:
**Convertible bonds:**
• Face value: $1,000,000
• Interest rate: 6%
• Conversion: 50 shares per $1,000 bond
• Tax rate: 21%

**Numerator adjustment:**
Interest expense: $1,000,000 × 6% = $60,000
After-tax: $60,000 × (1 - 0.21) = **$47,400 added back**

**Denominator adjustment:**
Bonds: $1,000,000 ÷ $1,000 = 1,000 bonds
Shares: 1,000 × 50 = **50,000 shares added**

**Dilutive test:** Does adding $47,400 / 50,000 reduce EPS?

sections.title: If-Converted: Convertible Preferred
sections.content:
**For convertible preferred stock:**

**Assume converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
+ Preferred dividends NOT subtracted (if converted, no dividend paid)

**Denominator adjustment:**
+ Shares issued upon conversion

**Key difference from bonds:** NO tax effect (dividends not tax-deductible)

sections.title: Antidilution Rule
sections.content:
**Critical rule:** Include security ONLY if DILUTIVE!

**Dilutive means:** Reduces EPS (or increases loss per share)

**Test each security:**
• Options: Exercise price < Average market price
• Convertibles: Incremental EPS < Basic EPS

**Incremental EPS for convertibles:**
$$\frac{\text{Numerator Effect}}{\text{Denominator Effect}}$$

**If incremental EPS > Basic EPS → ANTIDILUTIVE → Exclude!**

sections.title: Antidilution Example
sections.content:
**Basic EPS = $2.00**

**Convertible Bond A:**
• Interest saved (net): $30,000
• Shares from conversion: 20,000
• Incremental EPS: $30,000 ÷ 20,000 = **$1.50** ← DILUTIVE ✓

**Convertible Bond B:**
• Interest saved (net): $50,000
• Shares from conversion: 10,000
• Incremental EPS: $50,000 ÷ 10,000 = **$5.00** ← ANTIDILUTIVE ✗

**Include Bond A, EXCLUDE Bond B from diluted EPS**

sections.title: Order of Entry
sections.content:
**When multiple dilutive securities exist:**

**Rank by incremental EPS (lowest to highest):**
1. Include most dilutive security first
2. Recalculate EPS
3. Test next security against new EPS
4. Stop if next security is antidilutive

**This ensures maximum dilution is reported**

**Note:** Options/warrants have zero numerator effect, so their incremental EPS = 0 → Always enter first!

sections.title: Contingently Issuable Shares
sections.content:
**Shares contingent on future events:**

**For Basic EPS:**
• Include if ALL contingencies are met by year-end

**For Diluted EPS:**
• Include if contingencies would be met assuming year-end is end of contingency period

**Example contingencies:**
• Earnings targets (include if met at year-end level)
• Time-based (include if time has passed)
• Price-based (include if price met at year-end)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Loss Periods
sections.content:
**If company has a NET LOSS:**

**Basic EPS:** Use the loss (loss per share)

**Diluted EPS:** Usually equals Basic EPS!

**Why?** Adding shares to denominator when numerator is negative makes loss per share SMALLER (less negative) = ANTIDILUTIVE!

**Exception:** Convertible preferred would reduce the loss (don't subtract preferred dividend) → MAY be dilutive

**Rule:** In loss periods, most securities are excluded from diluted EPS

sections.title: EPS Presentation
sections.content:
**Required disclosure (face of income statement):**

**For each period presented:**
• Basic EPS from continuing operations
• Diluted EPS from continuing operations
• Basic EPS from net income
• Diluted EPS from net income

**If discontinued operations:**
• May present EPS from discontinued operations on face OR in notes

**Per share amounts for:**
• Each class of common stock
• Continuing operations and net income

sections.title: Comprehensive Example
sections.content:
**Year-end data:**
• Net income: $500,000
• Preferred dividends: $50,000
• Weighted avg shares: 100,000
• Options: 10,000 (exercise $30, market $50)
• Convertible bonds: Interest $40,000, tax 21%, conversion 15,000 shares

**Basic EPS:**
($500,000 - $50,000) ÷ 100,000 = **$4.50**

**Treasury stock (options):**
Incremental: 10,000 - (10,000 × $30 ÷ $50) = 4,000

**If-converted (bonds):**
Numerator add: $40,000 × 0.79 = $31,600

**Diluted EPS:**
($450,000 + $31,600) ÷ (100,000 + 4,000 + 15,000)
= $481,600 ÷ 119,000 = **$4.05**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Avg Shares
Stock dividends/splits: Retroactively adjust ALL periods
Treasury stock method for options: Shares issued - Shares repurchased at avg price
If-converted method: Add back interest (net of tax) or preferred dividends
Antidilution rule: Include only if reduces EPS; exclude if increases
Loss periods: Most securities are antidilutive
Order: Rank securities by incremental EPS; include most dilutive first

title: Statement of Cash Flows
description: Master the preparation and classification of cash flows using both direct and indirect methods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Statement of Cash Flows reveals the ACTUAL cash movements that the accrual-based income statement hides! Understanding operating, investing, and financing classifications—plus the indirect method adjustments—is critical. Cash flow questions appear on EVERY FAR exam!

sections.title: Three Categories of Cash Flows
rows: Cash from customers, interest received, dividends received
Cash to suppliers, employees, interest paid, taxes paid
Sale of PP&E, sale of investments, collection of loans made
Purchase of PP&E, purchase of investments, loans made
Repurchase of stock, repayment of debt, dividends paid

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: OIF
sections.content:
**"O-I-F: Operations pay for Investments through Financing"**

**Operating:** Day-to-day business (income statement related)
**Investing:** Long-term assets (buy/sell PP&E, investments)
**Financing:** Capital structure (debt and equity changes)

**Interest paid:** Operating (even though debt is financing!)
**Dividends paid:** Financing (distribution to owners)
**Dividends received:** Operating (investment income)

sections.title: Direct vs Indirect Method
sections.content:
**Two methods for presenting OPERATING activities:**

**Direct Method:**
• Shows actual cash receipts and payments
• Cash from customers, cash to suppliers, etc.
• More informative but rarely used
• If used, must provide reconciliation schedule

**Indirect Method:**
• Starts with net income
• Adjusts for non-cash items and working capital changes
• Most commonly used in practice
• FASB prefers direct; most companies use indirect

sections.title: Indirect Method Framework
sections.content:
**Start:** Net Income

**Adjust for non-cash expenses:**
+ Depreciation and amortization
+ Deferred taxes (increase)
+ Stock compensation expense
+ Losses (on asset sales, impairments)
- Gains (on asset sales)

**Adjust for working capital changes:**
+ Decrease in current assets (except cash)
- Increase in current assets
+ Increase in current liabilities
- Decrease in current liabilities

**= Cash from Operating Activities**

sections.title: Indirect Method Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Net income: $100,000
• Depreciation: $20,000
• Gain on sale of equipment: $5,000
• Increase in A/R: $15,000
