Charitable remainder trust: Donor gets income, NFP gets remainder
Charitable lead trust: NFP gets income, donor gets remainder
Gift annuity: NFP owns assets, pays fixed annuity to donor
Classified as 'with donor restrictions' until trust terminates

title: NFP Functional Expense Reporting
description: Master expense allocation and the Statement of Functional Expenses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Donors want to know how their money is spent! NFPs must report expenses by function (programs vs support) AND by nature (salaries, rent, etc.). The Statement of Functional Expenses shows this important information!

sections.title: Two Ways to Classify Expenses
sections.content:
**By Function (what the money does):**
• Program services
• Management and general
• Fundraising

**By Nature (what was purchased):**
• Salaries and benefits
• Rent and utilities
• Supplies
• Professional services
• Depreciation

sections.title: Statement of Functional Expenses
sections.content:
**Matrix format required:**

• Rows: Expenses by NATURE
• Columns: Expenses by FUNCTION
• Shows how each type of expense supports each activity

**Required for:**
• Voluntary health and welfare organizations (always)
• Other NFPs (encouraged, statement or notes)

**ASU 2016-14:** Enhanced disclosure requirements

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Functional Expense Matrix
sections.content:
**Think of a GRID:**

```
                  Programs  M&G  Fundraising
Salaries          $500K    $80K    $40K
Rent               $50K    $10K     $5K
Supplies           $20K     $3K     $2K
```

**Rows = NATURE (what bought)**
**Columns = FUNCTION (why bought)**
**Must allocate shared costs!**

sections.title: Expense Allocation
sections.content:
**Many costs must be allocated:**

**Direct costs:** Charge directly to function

**Shared costs:** Allocate using reasonable basis:
• Salaries → Time spent
• Rent → Square footage
• Utilities → Square footage
• Depreciation → Use or square footage

**Document allocation methodology!**

sections.title: Program Ratio
sections.content:
**Key metric donors watch:**

Program Ratio = Program Expenses / Total Expenses

**Higher = Better** (more going to mission)

**Typical expectations:**
• 75%+ considered good by many raters
• Below 50% raises concerns

**Watchdog groups like Charity Navigator use this!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Joint Costs
sections.content:
**Multi-purpose activities (e.g., educational mailing that also solicits):**

**Can allocate to BOTH program AND fundraising IF:**
1. Purpose criteria met (would do without solicitation)
2. Audience criteria met (selected for reasons other than ability to give)
3. Content criteria met (serves program/M&G function)

**ALL THREE criteria must be met to allocate to program!**
**Otherwise → 100% Fundraising**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Expenses reported by function AND by nature
Functions: Program, Management & General, Fundraising
Statement of Functional Expenses: Matrix format
Shared costs allocated using reasonable bases
Program ratio: Program expenses / Total expenses
Joint costs: Must meet all three criteria to allocate to program
Document allocation methodology

title: Healthcare Entity Accounting
description: Learn the unique accounting for hospitals and healthcare organizations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Healthcare entities—hospitals, clinics, and health systems—have unique accounting requirements, especially for patient service revenue and charity care! Understanding these special rules is essential for FAR healthcare questions!

sections.title: Types of Healthcare Entities
sections.content:
**Healthcare entities can be:**

• **For-profit:** Follow business GAAP
• **Government:** Follow GASB standards
• **Not-for-profit:** Follow FASB NFP standards

**Most FAR questions focus on NFP healthcare**

**Same functional expense reporting as other NFPs**

sections.title: Patient Service Revenue
sections.content:
**Revenue recognition (ASC 606):**

**Transaction price = Amount expect to collect**

• Start with gross charges
• Less: Contractual adjustments (insurance discounts)
• Less: Implicit price concessions (expected bad debts)
• = Net patient service revenue

**Report NET amount (single line)**

sections.title: Contractual Adjustments
sections.content:
**Differences between charges and insurance payments:**

**Example:**
Charges for procedure: $10,000
Insurance pays: $6,500
Contractual adjustment: $3,500

**Recorded as reduction of revenue, NOT expense**

**Third-party payers:**
• Medicare
• Medicaid
• Commercial insurance

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Revenue Components
sections.content:
**"GROSS minus REDUCTIONS = NET"**

**Gross patient service revenue**
- Contractual adjustments (insurance deals)
- Implicit price concessions (won't collect)
- Charity care (never expected to collect)
= **Net patient service revenue**

**Only report the NET!**

sections.title: Charity Care
sections.content:
**Services provided for free or reduced price:**

**ASU 2016-14 treatment:**
• NOT recorded as revenue
• NOT recorded as bad debt expense
• Disclosed in notes:
  - Policy for providing charity care
  - Quantified (e.g., at cost)

**Why not revenue?** Never expected to collect

sections.title: Healthcare Operating Statement
sections.content:
**Typical format:**

**Operating revenues:**
• Net patient service revenue
• Premium revenue (capitation)
• Other operating revenue

**Operating expenses:**
• By function (nursing, support, admin)

**Non-operating:**
• Investment income
• Contributions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Malpractice Claims
sections.content:
**Healthcare entities have significant malpractice exposure:**

**Record liability when:**
• Probable AND
• Reasonably estimable

**Uninsured claims:**
• Accrue expected losses
• Use actuarial estimates

**Insurance recoveries:**
• Asset only if probable

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Healthcare can be for-profit, government, or NFP
Patient service revenue: Net amount expected to collect
Contractual adjustments: Reduce revenue (insurance deals)
Charity care: Not revenue, disclosed in notes
Bad debts: Implicit price concession, reduces revenue
Malpractice: Accrue when probable and estimable
Most FAR questions focus on NFP healthcare

title: Defined Benefit Pension Plans
description: Master pension accounting including the PBO, plan assets, and net pension cost

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Pension accounting is one of the most complex topics on FAR! Understanding the components of pension expense, balance sheet presentation, and OCI impacts is essential. Many candidates struggle here—master this and you'll stand out!

sections.title: Key Pension Terms
sections.content:
**Projected Benefit Obligation (PBO):**
• Present value of ALL benefits earned to date
• Uses projected future salary increases
• PRIMARY measure used in US GAAP

**Plan Assets:**
• Fair value of investments set aside for pension
• Held in trust, separate from company

**Funded Status = Plan Assets - PBO**

sections.title: Funded Status on Balance Sheet
sections.content:
**Net Pension Asset or Liability:**

• **Overfunded (Assets > PBO):** Report net asset
• **Underfunded (PBO > Assets):** Report net liability

**Single amount on balance sheet = Funded status**

**No more minimum liability or intangible asset!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Pension Expense Components
sections.content:
**"SIR-AGE"** for Pension Cost:

**S**ervice cost (+)
**I**nterest cost (+)
**R**eturn on assets (-)
**A**mortization of prior service cost (+/-)
**G**ains and losses amortization (+/-)
**E**xpected, not actual return!

**Service and Interest ADD; Return SUBTRACTS**

sections.title: Service Cost
sections.content:
**Benefits earned by employees THIS YEAR**

• Calculated by actuary
• Based on pension formula
• Uses current salary levels projected forward

**This is the ONLY component in operating income!**

**All other components = Non-operating**

sections.title: Interest Cost
sections.content:
**Interest on the growing PBO:**

**Formula:** Beginning PBO × Discount Rate

**Think of it as:**
• PBO is a liability growing over time
• Interest accrues at the discount rate

**Increases pension expense**

sections.title: Expected Return on Plan Assets
sections.content:
**Expected earnings from investments:**

**Formula:** Beginning Plan Assets × Expected Return Rate

**Note:** Use EXPECTED return, not actual!

**Difference:** Actual vs Expected → OCI (deferred)

**Reduces pension expense (negative component)**

sections.title: Prior Service Cost
sections.content:
**When plan is amended retroactively:**

1. Measure change in PBO from amendment
2. Record in OCI immediately
3. Amortize to expense over remaining service period

**Example:** Company increases pension formula for past years → Increases PBO → Prior service cost

sections.title: Gains and Losses (Corridor Method)
sections.content:
**Gains/Losses arise from:**
• Actuarial assumption changes
• Difference between expected and actual returns

**Accumulated in OCI until:**
• Exceed the "corridor" (10% of greater of PBO or Assets)
• Then amortize excess over average remaining service

**This is the CORRIDOR approach - smooths volatility**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Expected vs Actual Return
sections.content:
**In pension expense: Use EXPECTED return!**

**Example:**
• Expected return: $100,000
• Actual return: $80,000
• Difference: $20,000 loss

**Pension expense uses:** $100,000 expected
**The $20,000 loss goes to:** OCI (deferred)

**Never use actual return in pension expense directly!**

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Pension Expense Calculation:**

• Service cost: $200,000
• Interest cost (PBO $2M × 5%): $100,000
• Expected return ($1.5M × 6%): ($90,000)
• Prior service amortization: $15,000
• Loss amortization: $10,000

**Total Pension Expense: $235,000**

**Journal Entry:**
Dr Pension Expense    $235,000
Dr OCI (various)      XXX
    Cr Pension Asset/Liability   XXX
    Cr Cash (contribution)       XXX

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Funded status = Plan assets - PBO (single B/S amount)
SIR-AGE: Service, Interest, Return, Amortizations
Service cost = ONLY component in operating income
Use EXPECTED return, not actual (difference to OCI)
Prior service cost: OCI, then amortize
Corridor: 10% of greater of PBO or assets
Overfunded = Asset; Underfunded = Liability

title: Basic Earnings Per Share (EPS)
description: Calculate and present basic EPS for simple capital structures

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: EPS is one of the most watched metrics by investors! Public companies must present EPS on the income statement. Understanding basic vs diluted EPS and the calculation nuances is heavily tested on FAR!

sections.title: Basic EPS Formula
sections.content:
**Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares**

**Numerator:** Income available to common shareholders
**Denominator:** Weighted average shares outstanding

**Must subtract preferred dividends!**

sections.title: Who Must Report EPS?
sections.content:
**Required for:**
• Public companies (SEC registrants)
• Companies planning to go public

**NOT required for:**
• Private companies
• Not-for-profits

**Presented ON the face of income statement**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Basic EPS
sections.content:
**"NIP over WAC"**

**N**et Income
**I**minus (less)
**P**referred dividends
_______________
**W**eighted
**A**verage
**C**ommon shares

**Top = what's for common; Bottom = how many common**

sections.title: Preferred Dividend Treatment
rows: Subtract FULL dividend (whether declared or not)

sections.title: Weighted Average Shares
sections.content:
**Calculate shares on time-weighted basis:**

**Example:**
• 100,000 shares for 6 months
• 150,000 shares for 6 months

**Weighted avg = (100,000 × 6/12) + (150,000 × 6/12)**
**= 50,000 + 75,000 = 125,000 shares**

**Use actual days or months for precision**

sections.title: Stock Dividends and Splits
sections.content:
**Treat as if occurred at BEGINNING of year!**

**Example:** 2-for-1 split on July 1
• January 1 shares: 100,000
• After split: 200,000

**Weighted average:** 200,000 for ENTIRE year

**Also RESTATE prior period EPS for comparability!**

sections.title: Treasury Stock
sections.content:
**Treasury shares reduce weighted average:**

**Example:**
• 1/1: 200,000 shares outstanding
• 4/1: Buy back 20,000 shares

**Weighted avg:**
(200,000 × 3/12) + (180,000 × 9/12)
= 50,000 + 135,000 = 185,000 shares

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Stock Dividends vs Repurchases
sections.content:
**Stock dividends/splits:** Apply RETROACTIVELY to entire year (and prior years)

**Stock repurchases:** Apply PROSPECTIVELY from purchase date

**Why different?**
• Splits/dividends don't change company value
• Repurchases are economic transactions

sections.title: EPS Presentation
sections.content:
**Income statement presentation:**

• EPS from continuing operations
• EPS from discontinued operations
• EPS for net income

**Both basic AND diluted EPS required**

**Show on face of income statement, not just notes**

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Calculate Basic EPS:**

• Net income: $500,000
• Preferred dividends (cumulative): $50,000
• Common shares 1/1: 100,000
• Issued 4/1: 20,000 additional
• 2-for-1 split on 9/1

**Step 1: Adjust for split**
• 1/1 shares: 100,000 × 2 = 200,000
• 4/1 issue: 20,000 × 2 = 40,000

**Step 2: Weighted average**
(200,000 × 3/12) + (240,000 × 9/12) = 230,000

**Step 3: Calculate EPS**
($500,000 - $50,000) / 230,000 = **$1.96**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Avg Shares
Cumulative preferred: Deduct whether declared or not
Weighted average: Time-weight share changes
Stock splits/dividends: Apply retroactively to entire year
Treasury stock purchases: Apply from purchase date forward
Required for public companies, on face of income statement
Restate prior periods for splits/dividends

title: Diluted Earnings Per Share
description: Calculate diluted EPS with convertibles, options, and other dilutive securities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Diluted EPS shows the 'worst case' scenario if all potentially dilutive securities converted. Understanding the if-converted and treasury stock methods is essential for FAR—these calculations appear frequently on the exam!

sections.title: What is Diluted EPS?
sections.content:
**Diluted EPS = Income available / (Weighted Avg + Potential Shares)**

**Shows impact of:**
• Convertible bonds
• Convertible preferred stock
• Stock options and warrants
• Contingent shares

**Always ≤ Basic EPS (cannot exceed basic)**

sections.title: Two Key Methods
rows: Add back interest (net of tax), add shares
Add back pref dividends, add shares

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Bonds
sections.content:
**Assume bonds converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
• Add back interest expense (net of tax)
• Interest wouldn't be paid if converted

**Denominator adjustment:**
• Add shares that would be issued

**Test:** Only include if DILUTIVE (reduces EPS)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: If-Converted
sections.content:
**"IF converted, what changes?"**

**Convertible BONDS:**
• No more interest expense = Income UP (add back)
• More shares = Shares UP

**Convertible PREFERRED:**
• No more preferred dividends = Income UP
• More common shares = Shares UP

**Both: Income goes UP, Shares go UP**

sections.title: If-Converted Method: Convertible Preferred
sections.content:
**Assume preferred converted at beginning of year:**

**Numerator adjustment:**
• Don't subtract preferred dividends
• (They wouldn't exist if converted)

**Denominator adjustment:**
• Add common shares from conversion

**No tax adjustment needed (dividends aren't deductible)**

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method: Options
sections.content:
**For options and warrants:**

1. Assume all options exercised
2. Company receives exercise price
3. Company buys back shares at average market price
4. NET new shares added to denominator

**Formula:**
Net shares = Options - (Options × Exercise Price / Avg Market Price)

**Or:** Options × (Market - Exercise) / Market

sections.title: Treasury Stock Method Example
sections.content:
**Options outstanding:**
• 10,000 options
• Exercise price: $20
• Average market price: $50

**Step 1:** Assume exercise: Receive $200,000 (10,000 × $20)

**Step 2:** Buy back shares: $200,000 / $50 = 4,000 shares

**Step 3:** Net new shares = 10,000 - 4,000 = **6,000 shares**

**Only these 6,000 added to denominator!**

sections.title: When Are Options Dilutive?
sections.content:
**Options are dilutive when:**
Exercise price < Average market price
(In-the-money options)

**Options are anti-dilutive when:**
Exercise price > Average market price
(Out-of-the-money options)

**Anti-dilutive securities are EXCLUDED!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Antidilutive Securities
sections.content:
**NEVER include antidilutive securities!**

**A security is antidilutive if:**
• Including it INCREASES EPS
• Convertible: Incremental EPS > Basic EPS
• Options: Exercise price > Market price

**Must test EACH security separately**

**Aggregate approach:** Order by most dilutive first

sections.title: Order of Inclusion (Aggregate Test)
sections.content:
**Steps for multiple dilutive securities:**

1. Calculate basic EPS
2. Rank securities by dilutive impact (most dilutive first)
3. Add one at a time
4. Stop when adding security would be antidilutive

**For convertibles:** Rank by incremental EPS
(Earnings adjustment / Share adjustment)

sections.title: Sample Comprehensive Calculation
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Net income: $1,000,000
• Preferred dividends: $100,000
• Basic shares: 200,000
• Convertible bonds: $500,000 face, 8% interest, converts to 20,000 shares
• Tax rate: 25%
• Stock options: 10,000 options at $30, market = $50

**Basic EPS:** ($1,000,000 - $100,000) / 200,000 = **$4.50**

**Convertible bond adjustment:**
• Interest add-back: $40,000 × (1-25%) = $30,000
• New shares: 20,000

**Options (Treasury Stock):**
• Net shares: 10,000 × ($50-$30)/$50 = 4,000

**Diluted EPS:**
($900,000 + $30,000) / (200,000 + 20,000 + 4,000)
= $930,000 / 224,000 = **$4.15**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Diluted EPS includes potentially dilutive securities
Convertibles: If-converted method (add back interest/dividends, add shares)
Options: Treasury stock method (only net new shares)
Options dilutive only when market > exercise price
NEVER include antidilutive securities
Test each security—stop when adding would increase EPS
Diluted EPS must be ≤ Basic EPS

title: Income Tax Accounting: Deferred Taxes
description: Master deferred tax assets, liabilities, and temporary differences

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Deferred tax accounting bridges the gap between GAAP and tax reporting! Understanding temporary differences, DTAs, DTLs, and valuation allowances is crucial for FAR. This topic appears in almost every exam!

sections.title: Temporary Differences
sections.content:
**Differences that create future taxable or deductible amounts:**

**Creates DTL (future taxable):**
• Accelerated tax depreciation
• Installment sale income
• Prepaid expenses (deducted for tax, not book)

**Creates DTA (future deductible):**
• Bad debt expense (allowance method)
• Warranty expense
• Accrued expenses not yet deductible

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DTA vs DTL
sections.content:
**"PAID less now → DTL; PAID more now → DTA"**

**DTL:** Tax paid NOW less than book expense
• Will pay MORE tax in future
• It's a LIABILITY

**DTA:** Tax paid NOW more than book expense
• Will pay LESS tax in future
• It's an ASSET (future benefit)

sections.title: Calculating Deferred Taxes
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Identify temporary differences
**Step 2:** Determine if each creates DTA or DTL
**Step 3:** Multiply by ENACTED tax rate
**Step 4:** Assess need for valuation allowance on DTAs

**Use the tax rate expected when difference reverses!**

sections.title: Common Temporary Differences
rows: Book full income, Tax when collected

sections.title: Permanent Differences (No Deferred Tax)
sections.content:
**Never reverse - affect current tax only:**

• Municipal bond interest (book income, not taxable)
• Life insurance premiums on executives
• Fines and penalties (book expense, not deductible)
• 50% meals deduction limit
• Dividends received deduction

**These affect effective tax rate, not deferred taxes**

sections.title: Journal Entry Approach
sections.content:
**Annual deferred tax entry:**

Dr Income Tax Expense (plug)
   Cr Income Tax Payable (current tax due)
   Cr Deferred Tax Liability (increase in DTL)
or
Dr Deferred Tax Asset (increase in DTA)

**Expense = Current tax +/- Changes in deferred taxes**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Rate Changes
sections.content:
**When tax rates change:**

• Adjust ALL existing deferred taxes
• Use NEW enacted rate
• Adjustment goes through income tax expense

**Example:** DTL of $100,000 at 35%
Rate changes to 21%
New DTL = Temp diff × 21%
Difference = Benefit (reduce expense)

sections.title: Balance Sheet Classification
sections.content:
**ALL deferred taxes are NONCURRENT:**

• Deferred Tax Assets → Noncurrent asset
• Deferred Tax Liabilities → Noncurrent liability

**Net within same tax jurisdiction:**
• Net DTA and DTL for same entity/jurisdiction
• Different jurisdictions shown separately

sections.title: Sample Calculation
sections.content:
**Year 1:**
• Book income: $500,000
• Taxable income: $400,000
• Difference: $100,000 (depreciation timing)
• Tax rate: 21%

**Current tax:** $400,000 × 21% = $84,000
**DTL created:** $100,000 × 21% = $21,000

**Entry:**
Dr Income Tax Expense    $105,000
    Cr Income Tax Payable    $84,000
    Cr Deferred Tax Liability $21,000

**Effective rate:** $105,000 / $500,000 = 21%

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Temporary differences reverse; Permanent differences don't
DTL = Will pay MORE tax later (tax < book now)
DTA = Will pay LESS tax later (tax > book now)
Use enacted rate expected when difference reverses
Rate changes: Adjust deferred taxes through expense
All deferred taxes are noncurrent on balance sheet
Net within same jurisdiction, separate different jurisdictions

title: Deferred Tax Asset Valuation Allowance
description: Determine when a valuation allowance is needed for deferred tax assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: A DTA is only valuable if the company will have future taxable income to use it! The valuation allowance is one of the most judgment-intensive areas in accounting—and a favorite FAR exam topic!

sections.title: What is a Valuation Allowance?
sections.content:
**Contra-asset reducing DTA to realizable amount**

**Required when:**
"More likely than not" (>50%) that some portion of DTA will NOT be realized

**Like an allowance for doubtful accounts—but for deferred taxes**

sections.title: Assessing Realizability
sections.content:
**Consider all available evidence:**

**Positive evidence (supports no VA needed):**
• Existing contracts/backlogs
• Strong earnings history
• Appreciated assets
• Deferred tax liabilities that will reverse

**Negative evidence (supports VA needed):**
• Cumulative losses in recent years
• History of unused carryforwards expiring
• Unsettled circumstances creating uncertainty

sections.title: Sources of Future Taxable Income
content: **Reversal of existing DTLs** in same jurisdiction
**Future taxable income** (exclusive of reversing differences)
**Tax planning strategies** that would create taxable income
**Carryback to prior years** (if available)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: "More Likely Than Not"
sections.content:
**MLTN = More than 50% likely**

**Question to ask:**
"Is it more likely than not that we WON'T use this DTA?"

**If YES → Need valuation allowance**
**If NO → No valuation allowance needed**

**Weight of evidence determines answer**

sections.title: Journal Entry for Valuation Allowance
sections.content:
**To establish or increase VA:**

Dr Income Tax Expense
    Cr Valuation Allowance (contra to DTA)

**To decrease VA (improved outlook):**

Dr Valuation Allowance
    Cr Income Tax Expense (benefit)

**VA is presented as offset to DTA on balance sheet**

sections.title: Cumulative Loss Test
sections.content:
**Three-year cumulative loss is significant negative evidence:**

• Very difficult to overcome
• Must have strong positive evidence to avoid VA
• Objectively verifiable

**But not automatic!**
• Consider nature of losses
• Unusual or nonrecurring?
• Future profitability reasonably expected?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Changing the VA
sections.content:
**VA is reassessed EVERY reporting period!**

**Company turns profitable:**
• Reassess need for VA
• May release (reduce) VA
• Benefit goes through income tax expense

**This can significantly impact earnings!**

**Disclosure of VA changes required**

sections.title: Example Scenario
sections.content:
**Company has:**
• DTA from NOL carryforward: $500,000
• Three years of losses (cumulative loss)
• Expect modest profits next 2 years
• DTL that will reverse: $200,000

**Analysis:**
• Negative evidence: Cumulative losses
• Positive evidence: DTL reversal
• Expected income insufficient

**Conclusion:** VA needed for portion not supported
VA = $500,000 - $200,000 = $300,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: VA needed when MLTN (>50%) DTA won't be realized
Consider all positive and negative evidence
Three-year cumulative loss = significant negative evidence
Sources: DTL reversals, future income, tax strategies, carrybacks
VA recorded against income tax expense
Reassess VA every period—changes go through expense
VA is contra-asset that reduces DTA on balance sheet

title: Revenue Recognition: Multiple Performance Obligations
description: Master allocation of transaction price to performance obligations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many contracts have multiple performance obligations! Understanding how to identify and allocate the transaction price is critical. This is one of the most complex areas of revenue recognition!

sections.title: Identifying Performance Obligations
sections.content:
**Two criteria (BOTH must be met):**

1. **Distinct:** Customer can benefit on its own or with readily available resources

2. **Separately identifiable:** Not highly interdependent or integrated with other promises

**If both met → Separate performance obligation**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Allocation Steps
sections.content:
**"STAR"** method:

**S**tandalone prices - Determine for each PO
**T**otal transaction price - Determine amount
**A**llocate based on relative SSP
**R**ecognize as each PO is satisfied

**Formula:**
Allocated = (SSP of PO / Total SSP) × Transaction Price

sections.title: Allocating Discounts
sections.content:
**General rule:** Allocate discount proportionally to all POs

**Exception - Allocate to specific POs if:**
• Entity regularly sells each PO separately, AND
• Observable evidence discount relates to only some POs, AND
• Items in bundle approximate standalone prices

**Variable consideration:** May allocate entirely to one PO if specifically relates

sections.title: Series of Distinct Goods/Services
sections.content:
**When treated as single PO:**

• Each distinct good/service is substantially the same, AND
• Same pattern of transfer to customer

**Examples:**
• Monthly cleaning services
• Daily transaction processing

**Simplifies accounting—single measure of progress**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Highly Interdependent
sections.content:
**NOT separately identifiable if:**

• Significant integration service
• One modifies or customizes another
• Highly interdependent

**Example:** Software + significant customization = ONE PO

**Don't default to assuming everything is separate!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Performance obligation: Distinct AND separately identifiable
Allocate transaction price based on relative SSP
SSP methods: Observable > Market > Cost-plus > Residual
Discounts generally allocated proportionally
Series of similar services may be single PO
Integration may combine promises into one PO
Recognize revenue as each PO satisfied

title: Revenue Recognition: Contract Modifications
description: Understand accounting for changes to revenue contracts

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contracts change all the time! Understanding how to account for modifications—new contract, cumulative adjustment, or prospective—is essential. The answer depends on whether goods/services are distinct!

sections.title: What Is a Contract Modification?
sections.content:
**Change to:**
• Scope (adding goods/services)
• Price (changing transaction price)
• Both

**Must be approved by parties**

**Examples:**
• Change orders
• Add-on services
• Price adjustments
• Quantity changes

sections.title: Three Accounting Models
rows: New contract, no adjustment to old

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Modification Flowchart
sections.content:
**Ask two questions:**

**Q1:** Are added goods/services distinct?
→ NO = Part of existing contract (catch-up)
→ YES = Go to Q2

**Q2:** Is price at standalone?
→ YES = Separate contract
→ NO = Terminate and create new

sections.title: Separate Contract Treatment
sections.content:
**Treat as new contract when:**

• Additional goods/services are distinct, AND
• Price increases by standalone selling price

**Result:**
• Original contract unaffected
• New contract starts fresh
• No cumulative adjustment

**Think of it as a completely new sale**

sections.title: Cumulative Catch-Up
sections.content:
**When modification changes measure of progress:**

**Steps:**
1. Revise total transaction price
2. Revise total estimated costs (if applicable)
3. Calculate new % complete
4. Apply to revised revenue
5. Recognize difference as adjustment

**Catch-up in period of modification!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Termination Model
sections.content:
**Terminate + New is NOT intuitive:**

**When goods are distinct but NOT at standalone price:**
• Allocate remaining transaction price
• Include modification consideration
• Recognize previously recognized revenue as starting point

**Remaining POs treated prospectively**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Modification: Change to scope, price, or both
Separate contract: Distinct goods + Standalone price
Terminate + New: Distinct goods but NOT standalone
Part of existing: Goods NOT distinct
Cumulative catch-up: Adjust in period of modification
Ask: Distinct? Then: Standalone price?
Document modification accounting at time of change

title: Inventory: Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value
description: Apply LCNRV testing and write-down accounting

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory must not be overstated! Understanding the lower of cost or NRV test ensures inventory is carried at the right amount. GAAP simplified this with ASU 2015-11!

sections.title: LCNRV Rule (Post-ASU 2015-11)
sections.content:
**For most inventory:**

**Lower of:**
• Cost (FIFO, LIFO, weighted average), OR
• Net Realizable Value (NRV)

**NRV = Estimated selling price − Costs to complete − Costs to sell**

**If NRV < Cost → Write down**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: LCNRV Simplified
sections.content:
**"Two numbers—pick the lower"**

• Cost (what you paid)
• NRV (what you'll get, net)

**No more ceiling/floor!**

**Exception:** LIFO and retail method still use old LCM approach

sections.title: Application Level
sections.content:
**Apply test to:**

• Individual items (most common)
• Categories of similar items
• Total inventory (rare)

**Be consistent!**

**Testing at item level:**
• Most conservative
• Catches individual write-downs
• Required for significant items

sections.title: Write-Down and Recovery
sections.content:
**Write-down entry:**

Dr Loss on Inventory Write-down (or COGS)
    Cr Inventory

**Recovery:**
• Can reverse write-down if NRV increases
• Only up to original cost
• Not above original carrying amount

**New cost basis = Written-down amount**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LIFO and Retail Method
sections.content:
**LCNRV does NOT apply to:**

• LIFO inventory
• Retail inventory method

**These STILL use old LCM:**
• Market = Replacement cost
• Subject to ceiling (NRV) and floor (NRV − profit)

**Read the question carefully!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: LCNRV: Lower of Cost or NRV (simplified from LCM)
NRV = Selling price − Completion costs − Selling costs
If NRV < Cost → Write down inventory
Apply at item, category, or total level
Write-down: Dr Loss/COGS, Cr Inventory
Can reverse write-down, but not above original cost
LIFO and retail method still use old LCM

title: Troubled Debt Restructuring (ASC 470-60)
description: Understand debtor and creditor accounting for restructured debt

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When borrowers face financial difficulty, debt often gets restructured! Understanding both debtor and creditor accounting is essential. ASU 2022-02 significantly changed these rules!

sections.title: What Is Troubled Debt?
sections.content:
**Creditor grants concession NOT otherwise granted:**

• Debtor experiencing financial difficulty
• Creditor agrees to modify terms
• Modification wouldn't be granted normally

**Examples:**
• Reduced interest rate
• Extended maturity
• Reduced principal
• Transfer of assets in settlement

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Settlement Gain
sections.content:
**Debtor gains when paying less:**

**Gain = Carrying value − Amount paid**

**If transferring asset:**
1. Asset sold at FV → G/L on sale
2. Debt settled at FV → G/L on settlement

**Two separate transactions!**

sections.title: Modification Accounting (Debtor)
sections.content:
**Post-ASU 2022-02:**

**Compare future cash flows to carrying amount:**

• If future payments < Carrying → Gain recognized
• If future payments ≥ Carrying → No immediate gain
  - Adjust effective interest rate
  - Prospective treatment

**No longer use old 10% test for distressed modifications**

sections.title: Creditor Accounting
sections.content:
**When restructuring occurs:**

• Measure loan at present value of future cash flows
• Use original effective interest rate
• Difference = Adjustment to allowance (or direct write-off)

**Consider impairment:**
• If collateral dependent: Fair value of collateral
• Expected credit loss model applies

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: ASU 2022-02 Changes
sections.content:
**Major changes for creditors:**

**OLD (TDR specific):**
• Separate TDR designation
• Different impairment model

**NEW (ASU 2022-02):**
• Eliminated TDR designation for creditors
• Use CECL expected loss model
• Enhanced disclosure requirements

**Debtor accounting: Less change**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: TDR: Creditor grants concession to distressed debtor
Settlement: Debtor recognizes gain for forgiveness
Asset transfer: Separate gain/loss on asset and debt
Modification: Compare future payments to carrying value
Creditor: Use CECL model, no separate TDR category
Present value calculations use original effective rate
ASU 2022-02 simplified creditor accounting

title: Software Development Costs
description: Understand capitalization of internal-use and for-sale software

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Software costs can be significant! Understanding when to capitalize vs expense is critical. Different rules apply to internal-use software vs software for sale!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Internal-Use Phases
sections.content:
**"PED" = Expense-Capitalize-Expense**

**P**reliminary → EXPENSE
**E**xploration, planning, evaluation

**D**evelopment → CAPITALIZE
Application development stage

**Post-implementation** → EXPENSE
Training, maintenance

sections.title: Internal-Use Software (ASC 350-40)
sections.content:
**Preliminary stage:** Expense
• Conceptual formulation
• Vendor evaluation
• Feasibility analysis

**Application development:** Capitalize
• Coding, testing
• Configuration
• Installation to hardware

**Post-implementation:** Expense
• Training, maintenance
• Data conversion (unless for new capabilities)

sections.title: Software for Sale (ASC 985-20)
sections.content:
**Before technological feasibility:** Expense all costs

**After technological feasibility:**
• Capitalize until ready for release
• Amortize over sales life

**Technological feasibility achieved when:**
• Detailed program design complete, OR
• Working model completed and confirmed

sections.title: Amortization
sections.content:
**Internal-use software:**
• Straight-line over useful life
• Generally 3-7 years

**Software for sale:**
• Greater of:
  - Straight-line over estimated life, OR
  - Revenue ratio (revenue/total expected revenue)

**Subject to impairment testing**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cloud Computing Costs
sections.content:
**Hosting arrangements (SaaS):**

**If NO software asset obtained:**
• Implementation costs = Service contract
• Capitalize and expense over contract term

**If software license obtained:**
• Treat as internal-use software
• Capitalize development costs

**Substance over form!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Internal-use: Capitalize application development stage
For sale: Capitalize after technological feasibility
Preliminary and post-implementation: Always expense
Technological feasibility: Detailed design or working model
Amortize over useful life or revenue pattern
Cloud computing: Generally expense unless license obtained
Subject to impairment testing

title: Asset Retirement Obligations (ARO)
description: Account for legal obligations to retire long-lived assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Many assets come with retirement obligations! Understanding how to recognize, measure, and update AROs is essential. Think oil wells, mines, nuclear plants—obligations to restore the environment!

sections.title: What Is an ARO?
sections.content:
**Legal obligation to retire long-lived asset:**

• Dismantling
• Removal
• Site restoration

**Arises from:**
• Existing laws/regulations
• Written or oral agreements
• Promises implied by past practice

**Must be legally enforceable**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: ARO Entry
sections.content:
**Initial recognition:**

Dr Asset (retirement cost)
    Cr ARO Liability (PV of obligation)

**Both increase at inception!**

**Over time:**
• Asset portion depreciated
• Liability accretes (interest)

sections.title: Subsequent Measurement
sections.content:
**Asset retirement cost:**
• Depreciate over asset life
• Systematic and rational method

**ARO Liability:**
• Accretion expense each period
• Uses credit-adjusted risk-free rate
• Accretion = Beginning liability × Rate

**Adjustments for estimate changes**

sections.title: Changes in Estimates
sections.content:
**When expected costs change:**

**Upward revision:**
• Increase liability at current rate
• Increase asset (new layer)
• Depreciate over remaining life

**Downward revision:**
• Decrease liability at original rate
• Decrease asset (not below zero)
• Debit expense if exceeds asset

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Settlement
sections.content:
**When obligation settled:**

**If settle for more than liability:**
Dr ARO Liability
Dr Loss on settlement
    Cr Cash

**If settle for less:**
Dr ARO Liability
    Cr Gain on settlement
    Cr Cash

**Timing differences can cause G/L**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: ARO: Legal obligation to retire long-lived asset
Initial: PV of expected costs → Liability AND Asset
Depreciate asset portion over useful life
Accrete liability using credit-adjusted rate
Upward revisions: New layer at current rate
Downward revisions: Reduce at original rate
Settlement difference → Gain or loss

title: Contingencies and Loss Accruals
description: Apply contingency recognition and disclosure rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contingencies can make or break financial statements! Understanding when to accrue, disclose, or ignore contingencies is essential. Litigation, warranties, guarantees—these are everywhere!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Probable = Accrue
sections.content:
**"Probable AND Estimable"**

To ACCRUE:
• **P**robable (likely to occur)
• **E**stimable (can measure)

**Both required!**

**Probable but NOT estimable:**
→ Disclose, don't accrue

sections.title: Measurement of Loss
sections.content:
**When amount is a range:**

**If best estimate within range exists:**
• Accrue best estimate

**If no best estimate:**
• Accrue MINIMUM of range
• Disclose full range

**Example:**
Range: $1M - $5M
No best estimate → Accrue $1M

sections.title: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**For accrued contingencies:**
• Nature of contingency
• Amount accrued

**For disclosed (not accrued):**
• Nature of contingency
• Estimate of possible loss (or state cannot estimate)

**May omit if prejudicial** (rare exception for litigation)

sections.title: Gain Contingencies
sections.content:
**Conservative treatment:**

• NEVER accrue gain contingencies
• May disclose if probable
• Avoid misleading implications

**Examples:**
• Pending lawsuits (plaintiff)
• Claims for insurance recovery
• Tax refund claims

**Recognize only when realized!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Warranty Costs
sections.content:
**Warranty = Always accrue!**

• Sale occurs → Probable warranty claims
• Past history → Estimable amount

**Accrue at time of sale:**
Dr Warranty Expense
    Cr Warranty Liability

**When claims paid:**
Dr Warranty Liability
    Cr Cash/Parts

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Accrue if: Probable AND Estimable
Disclose if: Probable but not estimable, or Reasonably possible
Remote contingencies: Generally no action
Range with no best estimate: Accrue minimum
Gain contingencies: NEVER accrue, may disclose
Warranties: Always accrue (probable and estimable)
May omit disclosure if prejudicial (litigation)

title: Equity Method Investments
description: Master accounting for significant influence investments

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Equity method applies to investments with significant influence! Understanding how to apply it—including basis differences and intercompany transactions—is critical for FAR!

sections.title: When to Use Equity Method
sections.content:
**Significant influence presumed at 20-50% ownership:**

**Indicators of significant influence:**
• Board representation
• Policy-making participation
• Material transactions
• Interchange of personnel
• Technical dependence

**Can rebut presumption either direction**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Investment Balance
sections.content:
**"CIDE"** changes investment:

**C**ost (initial investment)
**I**ncome (% of investee earnings)
**D**ividends (received)
**E**xcess amortization (basis differences)

**Investment = Cost + Income − Dividends − Excess**

sections.title: Basis Differences
sections.content:
**When cost ≠ % of book value:**

**Excess over book value:**
• Allocate to identifiable assets (FV > BV)
• Remainder = Goodwill

**Amortization:**
• Depreciable assets → Amortize over useful life
• Inventory → Expense when sold
• Goodwill → Not amortized (but test for impairment)

**Reduces equity income each period**

sections.title: Intercompany Transactions
sections.content:
**Eliminate investor's % of unrealized profit:**

**Downstream (investor → investee):**
• Eliminate % of profit from investor's income

**Upstream (investee → investor):**
• Eliminate % of profit from equity income

**Profit realized when asset sold to third party**

sections.title: Investment Below Zero
sections.content:
**When losses exceed investment:**

• Stop recognizing losses at zero
• Continue recognizing if:
  - Guaranteed investee debt
  - Made loans/advances
  - Committed to provide support

**Apply losses to other investments in investee first**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Change in Level of Influence
sections.content:
**If drop below 20%:**
• Stop equity method
• Carry investment at FV (usually)
• Use new basis going forward

**If acquire additional and exceed 50%:**
• Now consolidated
• Remeasure prior investment to FV
• Recognize gain/loss

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Equity method: 20-50% ownership (significant influence)
Record % of income; dividends reduce investment
Basis differences: Allocate excess to assets, amortize
Goodwill: Not amortized, test for impairment
Intercompany: Eliminate % of unrealized profit
Losses limited to investment plus committed amounts
Change in influence: Stop/start method, remeasure

title: Consolidation: Noncontrolling Interests
description: Account for NCI in consolidated financial statements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When parent owns less than 100%, noncontrolling interest exists! Understanding how to present NCI in consolidated statements—and what gets allocated to it—is essential for FAR!

sections.title: What Is NCI?
sections.content:
**Noncontrolling interest:**

• Portion of subsidiary NOT owned by parent
• Equity component of consolidated F/S
• Presented within equity, separate from parent's equity

**Example:**
Parent owns 80% of Sub
NCI = 20% of Sub

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: NCI in Equity
sections.content:
**NCI is EQUITY, not liability!**

**Presented:**
• Within equity section
• Separate line from parent's equity

**Allocate to NCI:**
• % of subsidiary income/loss
• % of OCI
• Dividends to NCI shareholders

sections.title: Consolidated Income Statement
sections.content:
**Bottom of income statement:**

Net income: $X
  Net income attributable to NCI: $(Y)
  Net income attributable to Parent: $X-Y

**NCI gets their %:**
• Of subsidiary's net income
• After fair value adjustments
• Including excess amortization

sections.title: Changes in Ownership
sections.content:
**Parent buys more (stays in control):**
• Equity transaction (APIC adjustment)
• No gain/loss recognized
• NCI decreases

**Parent sells some (maintains control):**
• Equity transaction (APIC adjustment)
• No gain/loss recognized
• NCI increases

**Deconsolidation (loses control):**
• Gain/loss recognized in P&L

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Losses Exceeding NCI
sections.content:
**Under current GAAP:**

• Losses allocated to NCI even if goes negative
• NCI can have negative balance
• Parent absorbs all losses historically is OLD rule

**NCI shares in ALL income and losses!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NCI: Portion of subsidiary not owned by parent
Presented in equity, separate from parent's equity
Full goodwill: Measure NCI at fair value
Allocate % of income, loss, and OCI to NCI
Changes maintaining control: Equity transactions
Loss of control: Gain/loss recognized
Losses can make NCI balance negative

title: Statement of Cash Flows: Operating Section
description: Master direct and indirect methods for operating activities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The operating section is the most complex part of the cash flow statement! Understanding both indirect and direct methods—and the adjustments needed—is essential for FAR!

sections.title: Two Methods
sections.content:
**Indirect method (most common):**
• Start with net income
• Adjust for non-cash items
• Adjust for working capital changes

**Direct method:**
• Show gross cash receipts/payments
• Requires supplemental reconciliation

**Same total CFO—different presentation**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Working Capital
sections.content:
**"IOLA"** for current assets:

**I**ncrease in asset = **O**ut (subtract)
**L**oss of asset = **A**dd

**Opposite for liabilities:**
• Increase in liability = ADD
• Decrease in liability = SUBTRACT

sections.title: Direct Method Categories
sections.content:
**Cash receipts:**
• Cash collected from customers
• Interest received
• Dividends received

**Cash payments:**
• Cash paid to suppliers
• Cash paid to employees
• Interest paid
• Income taxes paid

sections.title: Converting to Direct Method
sections.content:
**Cash from customers:**
Sales − ↑A/R (or + ↓A/R)

**Cash to suppliers:**
COGS + ↑Inventory − ↑A/P

**Cash for expenses:**
Expenses + ↑Prepaids − ↑Accrued liabilities

**Use T-accounts to trace!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest and Dividends
sections.content:
**Under U.S. GAAP:**

**Operating:**
• Interest paid
• Interest received
• Dividends received

**Financing:**
• Dividends PAID

**IFRS allows more flexibility in classification!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Indirect: Net income → Adjustments → CFO
Add back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
Remove gains (subtract), add back losses
Current asset increase = Subtract; Decrease = Add
Current liability increase = Add; Decrease = Subtract
Direct: Gross receipts and payments shown
Interest paid/received = Operating (GAAP)

title: Government Fund Accounting Basics
description: Master the fund structure and measurement focus

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Government accounting uses FUNDS to track resources! Understanding the fund categories, measurement focus, and basis of accounting is essential. This is a major FAR topic!

sections.title: Fund Categories
rows: General, Special Revenue, Capital Projects, Debt Service, Permanent
Custodial, Pension, Investment Trust, Private-purpose Trust

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: "GR CEPS DIP"
sections.content:
**Governmental funds:**

**G**eneral
**R**evenue (Special)
**C**apital Projects
**E**mergency... wait, no:
**D**ebt Service
**P**ermanent

**"Go Run CoPs, Dr. Peppers"**

sections.title: Measurement Focus and Basis
sections.content:
**Governmental funds:**
• **Focus:** Current financial resources
• **Basis:** Modified accrual
• Report assets that can be spent

**Proprietary & Fiduciary:**
• **Focus:** Economic resources
• **Basis:** Full accrual
• Like commercial accounting

sections.title: Modified Accrual Basis
sections.content:
**Revenues recognized when:**
• Measurable AND Available
• Available = Collected within 60 days

**Expenditures recognized when:**
• Liability incurred (usually)
• Exceptions: Debt service, claims/judgments

**No depreciation in governmental funds!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: General Fund
sections.content:
**General Fund is UNIQUE:**

• Only fund that can have UNASSIGNED balance
• Must be used for activities not assigned elsewhere
• All governments MUST have General Fund

**Other funds can have NEGATIVE assigned balance = Unassigned**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three fund categories: Governmental, Proprietary, Fiduciary
Governmental: Modified accrual, current resources
Proprietary/Fiduciary: Full accrual, economic resources
Modified accrual: Measurable AND available
Fund balance: Nonspendable → Unassigned hierarchy
Only General Fund can have unassigned balance
No depreciation in governmental funds

title: Government-wide Financial Statements
description: Understand full accrual conversion and dual perspective

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Government-wide statements show the BIG PICTURE! Understanding the conversion from fund-level modified accrual to government-wide full accrual is a key exam skill!

sections.title: Government-wide Statements
sections.content:
**Two required statements:**

**1. Statement of Net Position**
• Assets + Deferred outflows
• − Liabilities − Deferred inflows
• = Net Position

**2. Statement of Activities**
• Program revenues vs expenses
• Net (expense) revenue format

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Full Accrual
sections.content:
**"GLAAD" Adjustments:**

**G**apital assets (capitalize)
**L**ong-term liabilities (add)
**A**ccrued revenues (beyond 60 days)
**A**ccrued expenses
**D**epreciation (add expense)

**Modified → Full accrual**

sections.title: Net Position Categories
sections.content:
**Three categories:**

**1. Net investment in capital assets**
• Capital assets, net of depreciation
• Less related debt

**2. Restricted**
• External restrictions

**3. Unrestricted**
• Everything else

sections.title: Statement of Activities Format
sections.content:
**Net (expense) format:**

Expenses
− Program revenues (charges, grants)
= Net expense

+ General revenues (taxes, interest)
= Change in net position

**Separates governmental from business-type**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Internal Service Funds
sections.content:
**ISF on government-wide statements:**

• Report with GOVERNMENTAL activities
• NOT with business-type activities
• Because ISF primarily serves governmental

**Enterprise funds = Business-type activities**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Government-wide: Full accrual, economic resources
Two statements: Net Position, Activities
Convert fund statements → Government-wide
Add capital assets, long-term debt, depreciation
Net position: Invested in capital, Restricted, Unrestricted
Internal service funds → Governmental activities
Net expense format shows cost of services

title: Not-for-Profit Financial Statements
description: Master NFP financial statement requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: NFP financial statements are different from business! Understanding net asset classes, statement formats, and donor restrictions is essential for FAR. Major testing area!

sections.title: Required Financial Statements
sections.content:
**Four required statements:**

1. **Statement of Financial Position** (like B/S)
2. **Statement of Activities** (like I/S)
3. **Statement of Cash Flows** (similar)
4. **Statement of Functional Expenses** (unique!)

**All follow ASC 958**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Two Classes Now
sections.content:
**OLD (3 classes):**
• Unrestricted
• Temporarily restricted
• Permanently restricted

**NEW (2 classes - ASU 2016-14):**
• **Without** donor restrictions
• **With** donor restrictions

**Simpler!**

sections.title: Statement of Activities
sections.content:
**Shows:**
• Revenues (by net asset class)
• Expenses (only without restrictions)
• Net releases from restrictions
• Change in net assets

**Expenses always in "without restrictions"**

**Contributions increase restricted or unrestricted**

sections.title: Statement of Functional Expenses
sections.content:
**Shows expenses by:**

**Function:**
• Program services
• Supporting activities (management, fundraising)

**Nature:**
• Salaries, rent, depreciation, etc.

**Matrix format showing both**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Expense Classification
sections.content:
**Joint costs allocation:**

When activity has multiple purposes (program + fundraising):

• Must meet 3 criteria to allocate
• Purpose, Audience, Content tests
• If all three not met: 100% to fundraising

**Fundraising ratio is watched closely!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four NFP statements required
Two net asset classes: With/Without restrictions
Expenses always shown without restrictions
Releases shift from restricted → unrestricted
Functional expenses: Program vs Supporting
Joint cost allocation: 3 criteria required
Statement of functional expenses is unique to NFP

title: NFP Contributions and Promises
description: Apply contribution recognition and pledges receivable

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contributions are the lifeblood of NFPs! Understanding when to recognize revenue, how to handle promises, and conditional vs unconditional gifts is critical!

sections.title: Contribution Recognition
sections.content:
**Unconditional contributions:**
• Recognize when received/promised
• Either restricted or unrestricted

**Conditional contributions:**
• Record when condition is met
• Before that: No revenue or receivable

**Key distinction on exam!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Barrier Test
sections.content:
**"Barrier" = Must DO something**

**Conditional (barrier exists):**
• "Raise matching funds"
• "Achieve certain outcomes"

**Unconditional (restriction only):**
• "Use for scholarships"
• "Spend after Jan 1, 2025"

**Restriction = How to use**
**Condition = Must earn it**

sections.title: Promises to Give (Pledges)
sections.content:
**Unconditional pledge:**
• Record at fair value
• Multi-year: Present value
• Allow for uncollectibles

**Recognition:**
• < 1 year: Without restrictions (unless restricted)
• > 1 year: With restrictions (time restriction implied)

**As time passes: Release restriction**

sections.title: Contributed Services
sections.content:
**Only recognize if:**

• Create/enhance nonfinancial asset, OR
• Require specialized skills AND
  Would be purchased if not donated

**Examples:**
✓ Attorney provides legal services
✓ Electrician wires building
✗ Volunteer shelving books
✗ General administrative help

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Donor-Imposed
sections.content:
**Only DONOR can impose restrictions!**

• Board cannot restrict net assets
• Board can "designate" but it's still unrestricted
• Called "board-designated"

**Disclosure required but NOT separately classified!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Unconditional: Recognize immediately at FV
Conditional: Barrier + Right of return must be met
Multi-year pledges: PV, time-restricted
Release restrictions when met
Contributed services: Specialized skills required
Board designation ≠ Donor restriction
Conditional: No revenue until condition met

title: Bonds Payable: Premium and Discount
description: Master bond issuance and effective interest method

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Bonds are major financing! Understanding premium vs discount, effective interest method, and interest expense calculation is essential. Heavily tested on FAR!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Rate Relationship
sections.content:
**"Investors want MARKET rate"**

**High stated rate = Premium:**
Investors pay MORE for higher interest

**Low stated rate = Discount:**
Investors pay LESS for lower interest

**Market rate ALWAYS wins!**

sections.title: Effective Interest Method
sections.content:
**Each period:**

**Interest expense** = Carrying value × Market rate

**Cash payment** = Face value × Stated rate

**Difference = Amortization**

• Premium: Expense < Cash → Reduce premium
• Discount: Expense > Cash → Reduce discount

sections.title: Amortization Entries
sections.content:
**Premium amortization:**
Dr Interest expense (smaller)
Dr Premium on bonds (difference)
    Cr Cash (stated × face)

**Discount amortization:**
Dr Interest expense (larger)
    Cr Discount on bonds (difference)
    Cr Cash (stated × face)

sections.title: Carrying Value Over Time
sections.content:
**Premium bonds:**
• Carrying value DECREASES
• Approaches face value at maturity

**Discount bonds:**
• Carrying value INCREASES
• Approaches face value at maturity

**At maturity: CV = Face value (always)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Interest Expense Trend
sections.content:
**Interest expense changes each period!**

**Premium:**
• CV decreases → Interest expense decreases

**Discount:**
• CV increases → Interest expense increases

**Expense = CV × Market rate**
**CV changes → Expense changes**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Market rate < Stated = Premium (pay more)
Market rate > Stated = Discount (pay less)
Interest expense = CV × Market rate
Cash paid = Face × Stated rate
Premium: Expense decreases over time
Discount: Expense increases over time
At maturity: CV = Face value

title: Deferred Revenue and Contract Liabilities
description: Understand performance obligations and timing

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Cash received before performance creates a contract liability! Understanding when to recognize versus defer revenue is fundamental to proper financial reporting!

sections.title: Contract Liability Definition
sections.content:
**Contract liability (deferred revenue):**

• Entity received consideration
• Performance obligation not yet satisfied
• Obligation to transfer goods/services

**Examples:**
• Advance payments
• Subscriptions received in advance
• Gift cards

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Earned vs Received
sections.content:
**Revenue recognition:**

**Cash received ≠ Revenue earned**

**Must satisfy performance obligation!**

**Cash first → Contract liability**
**Obligation first → Contract asset**

sections.title: Gift Card Breakage
sections.content:
**Some gift cards never redeemed:**

**If breakage estimated:**
• Recognize revenue proportionally
• As customer redeems other cards

**If breakage not estimable:**
• Recognize when likelihood of redemption is remote

**Still a contract liability until recognized!**

sections.title: Customer Prepayments
sections.content:
**Common scenarios:**

• Magazine subscriptions
• Maintenance contracts
• Season tickets
• Rent received in advance

**Recognize:**
• Over time as services provided
• At point in time when goods delivered

**Match revenue to performance!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Balance Sheet Classification
sections.content:
**Current vs Noncurrent:**

• Satisfy within 12 months = Current
• Satisfy after 12 months = Noncurrent

**Multi-year contracts:**
• Allocate between current and noncurrent
• Based on when obligations will be satisfied

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Contract liability: Cash received, obligation pending
Recognize revenue when obligation satisfied
Gift card breakage: Proportional or remote method
Match revenue recognition to performance
Current/Noncurrent based on timing
Advance payment is NOT revenue
Contract liability ≠ Accounts payable
